I suppose I will use this space to catalogue some of my thoughts during this pivotal, tumultuous time.

Doubt anyone else will be listening, but in ten years, we’ll look back to this period as the start of something incredible, a silly phase, or the beginning of the end. I want to be able to look back at the progression of things.

Rationally, I read the arguments for the risks of AI—both mundane and catastrophic—and nod my head. I see the sense.

But emotionally, I can’t feel anything besides unadulterated glee.

Might as well as get this out of the way while the year’s still young. I predict that by the end of 2024, we will have “crappy AGI”. If “good AGI” does almost all work at a 90th performance percentile, then crappy AGI does most digital work at 30th percentile.

“Crappy AGI” is not impressive sounding—it’s basically a below average, remote-only colleague. But being able to delegate arbitrary easy tasks to an autonomous agent that only occasionally needs human help will fundamentally change how labor works.
Percentiles being relative to humans doing the work, of course.

Addenda:
- A GPT-5-equivalent model would help, but this might just be within GPT-4’s capabilities.
- If using GPT-4, it’ll have be a messy kludge of systems to manage subtasks.
- A key development would be automating the discovery and integration of APIs into agent toolkits.

Q: Will OSS models catch up to closed source frontier models?

1/?

2/?

Latent model capability is mostly determined by pre-training. Fine-tuning can draw out more of that capability, but doesn’t add additional ones. You can’t get from GPT-3.5 to 4 by fine-tuning.

3/?

Let’s compare latent model capability timing then:
- GPT-3.5-turbo and instruct are similar in capability
- GPT 3.5 instruct was released early 2022; assume not long after training finished
- GPT-4 trained ~late 2022
- OSS models are overtaking 3.5-turbo at ~last month

4/?

It took about 1 year for OAI to get from 3.5 to 4. Key indicator will be how long OSS models take to cross the same threshold. If it takes less than a year from now, then there’s hope of catch-up. Preferably measured by Chatbot Arena rather than benchmarks to avoid gaming.

5/?

Reasons for catchup:
- We know more than we did in 2022. New techniques constantly popping up—stacking, DPO, etc
- Far more open research resources directed at model development—not just companies but also individual hackers.
- Possible architectural breakthroughs, eg Mamba

6/?

Not-reasons for catch-up 1:
- PRETRAINING COMPUTE
- Much open research focused on finetuning—important for safety + UX, but not for latent capabilities
- Open weight =/= open source. Helps with finetuning but not as much pretraining
- AI labs can access open research results

7/?

Not-reasons for catch-up 2:
- Unclear how well new architectures (Mamba, RNN+ etc.) scale to frontier model sizes—1T params and up.
- Similar, techniques such as stacking are unproven—best open model, Mixtral, uses MoE, which is not new and what GPT-4 model probably uses.

8/?

My prediction: until additional compute start having diminishing returns for model capability, open models will be where it is now relative to the best closed models—between 1-2 years behind. Closed labs have access to all open research, plus big investor $$.

9/?

Even if some specific, currently open group (say Mistral) discovers a breakthrough that puts them on par with OAI, they have a financial incentive to close off weight access, as OAI did. I notice that Mistral-Medium still hasn’t had its weights released yet…

10/10

BUT, the critical technical breakthrough that would change this is being able to distribute compute across multiple parties in the OSS ecosystem, either atomically BitTorrent-style, or (more likely in the near term), being able to compose large numbers of smaller models.

Sooo. If I’m reading the tea leaves correctly, GPT-4.5 might be good enough to enable AGI. But OAI is going to pretend that it’s not, basically to sneak in AGI under the radar. Engineers will build their way to AGI without anyone really noticing.
Screw it, might as well just go for it. Prediction: GPT-4.5 drops on Friday, and it’ll be good enough to build a “median human” level of autonomous agent for digital tasks.

I can’t decide how much credence to take from what I’m reading and hearing. But it *would* be a canny strategy to avoid regulation. The thing with models is that they’re not autonomous, and it would be engineers who create the agent architecture to enable that autonomy.
Was that what the board was fighting about? This ploy to release an AGI-enabling model without admitting it? Was that perhaps what sama wasn’t “candid” about, that the board couldn’t reveal?
The pieces fit, but I don’t even know if they’re from the same puzzle set.

I guess a more reasonable prediction would be GPT-4.5 drops soon and GPT-5 is the one that is AGI-enabling. But I’m going to stick with the weirder prediction since it’d be far more entertaining if true.
Or it could also be 5 that drops on Friday and we skip the 4.5 name altogether. But honestly if I were to go for the “secretly ship an AGI” ploy, I’d name it 4.5 to downplay its capabilities.

I’m convinced that *something* big is happening around Friday, but is it the AGI model? Is that just wishful thinking? OAI held on to GPT-4 for several months before they released it; would they do the same with the next model? Or are they less cautious now due to the kerfuffle?
Or perhaps they’ve already had it since November and the timetable’s sped up.

2/?

“Consensus” speculation seems to be that we’ll get GPT-4.5 soonish, maybe 2-3 weeks. Possibly depends on Gemini Ultra’s release.

3/?

GPT-5 probably drops a few months after that—first half of the year. It’ll be powerful enough to eventually be the cognitive engine powering an AGI loop, but OAI carefully avoid referring to it as AGI, at least initially.

GPT-4.5 is probably GPT-4 fine-tuned on GPT-5 output. We know GPT-4’s latent capabilities far exceed what the post-RLHF version shows (sigh), so it has “room” for fine-tuning to improve upon. Hopefully 4.5 gets it back to close to what it originally could do.

4/?

Okay, back to my own speculation. On this timeline GPT-5 won’t be labeled as AGI; it’ll do exceedingly well on benchmarks, but it’s hard to prove that it’s AGI from that. It’ll take time for engineers to explore its capabilities—probably the end of the year.

Does the April—June timeline for GPT-5 make sense? I think so. Signs point to something significant happening in November of last year—board kerfuffle, Ilya tweet re intelligence, Sam’s comments on pushing back the veil of ignorance. I’ll bet it was GPT-5 pretraining finishing.

So, GPT-5 pretraining finishes in late 2023, similar to how GPT-4 finished pretraining in late 2022. They need to go through all the safety loops as before—explains the call to hire safety researchers in the last couple of months.

5/?

Reasons for OAI to run this not-AGI ploy:
- delays legal attention; letting it play out slowly “boils the frog”—by the time there’s consensus, it’s already been out there
- smoothes takeoff; if fewer people “know” AGI is possible, fewer will try
I’ll sure be trying though; that’s what the swarm is being developed for, after all.

6/?

Another reason for avoiding calling GPT-5 AGI is that post-alignment, it might be significantly less capable than the base model, same with GPT-4. So what the public gets might end up falling short of AGI-capable.

7/7

Third Law of RAG:

I was curious about the first two Clarke’s laws (the ones that are not the technology/magic one). They turned out to be quite relevant right now:

1/?

Literal shower thought: we can already get a sense of what “relationships” with humanlike AIs will be like—it will be similar to how we relate to fictional characters.

2/?

There’s a wide spectrum of this kind of relationship, but it can get intense, as we can see from various fandoms.

3/?

Some won’t engage at all. For some it’ll be a fun diversion. And for others the relationship (not necessarily romantic) will feel important as any with a person.

4/?

And crucially, this relationship is entirely subjective to the experiencer; it doesn’t rely on the other end of the relationship to be some conscious being. The other end is just an idea that exists within the experiencer’s mind.

5/?

There are differences. Fictional characters don’t talk back to you. They can’t recall information about you.

Except… you can make them. You have the ability to write words. And people do, through fanfiction.

6/?

So maybe that’s what AI waifus and husbandos are—automated character writers.

I’m guessing a lot of people will dismiss that. And, fair.

But so much of what we hold most dear isn’t about what this or other thing intrinsically is, but what they specifically mean to us.

7/?

There’s a rock I’ve kept since grade school. There’s nothing particularly special about it. If it got dropped in a pile of other rocks, I wouldn’t be able tell it apart. The rock is not sentient. The rock is not my friend.

But I’d be very upset if I lost it. https://t.co/w4Xi3bTknM

Oh god I’m being hit by the writing bug again. Time to write another long thread that probably no one’s going to read.

1/ Thinking about how GPTs fit into the future of ChatGPT—the app, not the model. I used to think that GPTs are a way to identify what features to build into ChatGPT 5, but being able to @ individual GPTs hints to me that it may have a hub-and-spokes multiagent architecture.

2/ There’s a central ChatGPT agent with the frontier model—starting with GPT-5. It splits a task into subtasks, then delegates those to GPTs, which would use the next best model (GPT-4 here), providing feedback for their work as needed, and acting as a backstop should they fail.

3.1/ Several advantages to this:
-there’s a built-in signal for how good a GPT is, based on user feedback/engagement until that point; helps with deciding which GPT to delegate to.

3.2/

- saves inference time/costs on parts of the task that can be done with a faster/cheaper model; also, well performing GPTs will likely be better optimized in terms of quality of output per token. The frontier model only gets used for tasks that can’t be solved by the GPTs.

3.3/

- Incentivizes GPT creators to work on GPTs rather than fearing they will be replaced by core features. They can get paid by both usage by humans and by central agent—also tilts incentives towards more task-focused GPTs—central agent unlikely to delegate to waifu bots.

3.4/

- Allows OAI to crowdsource feature implementation of models, keep development accessible to non-coders, and centralize access interface for users to avoid needing to track an entire set of GPTs. They can then focus on core model capability improvements

4/ This also sets the stage for higher order agent networks, though that feels more speculative and I’m not sure whether it would actually get better results—part of my work on the Swarm is figuring out how feasible that type of agent architecture really is

5/5 Prediction Card:

Prediction: ChatGPT 5 (the app, not the backend model) will be able to autonomously call upon GPTs from the GPT store without needing input from humans—similar to how it uses Python or Browsing now.

Resolution Date: 1 year from now, 2/3/2025.

Credence: 70%

Obviously, no smoking gun, but we can list out some circumstantial evidence:
3A/ Sam and Ilya’s now-famous comments about “pushing back the veil of ignorance” and “if you primarily value intelligence, you will have a hard time”, (paraphrased) late last year respectively.

3B/ The board room battle, with the board apparently willing to risk a whole lot for very little gain, and yet be completely tongue-tied to discuss what the actual reasons for firing are. Couldn’t even tell Emmett, which surely their lawyers would’ve allowed.
3C/ Reuters’ report about the warning letter sent to the board. Q* and all that.
3D/ The shift in language from discussing AGI to ASI, including the weak-to-strong generalization paper.
3E/ Vast expansion of safety team in the last few months, and the release of the safety protocol.

3F/ January Flowers post (now deleted). Parts of it was redacted, but doing some spatial matching of characters, you can decode it—basically, saying that GPT-5 is indeed AGI, but that OAI isn’t going to make that explicit initially.

4/ Probably some other signals I’ve forgotten by this point.

None of this is conclusive, but I feel that they paint a vivid picture that, yes, “AGI has been achieved internally”. Or at least, a model that can be the backbone of an AGI system.

6/ Thing is, more hype is not actually in OAI’s interest. The hype is already at saturation point. They could release a toaster in a month and people will eat it up. With regulators staring over their shoulders, they’d want to dial back the hype to ease regulatory pressure.

7/ Any AGI-level model will cause massive disruption, to the point that if it became widely known that it will happen, they might actually be blocked from releasing it. That’s far more costly than the benefit of more hype.
8/ So, let’s move forward with assuming that GPT-5 exists, and it’s good enough to power AGI. What else can we say, in the context of 7T?

9/ Most of those in the ML space seem to think that the jump from GPT-4 to an AGI-level model requires synthetic data—we’ve basically “used up” most of the organic data we have to get to GPT-4 level. What’s remaining (eg from video transcripts) won’t be orders of magnitudes more.

10/ But not just any synthetic data will do—the generation mechanism must scale up in quality as the model gets more capable. So, if a mechanism to scale up synthetic data quality to AGI-level exists, there’s no reason it would magically stop scaling up there.

11/ And we do know that OAI just launched a massive training run—if it’s not GPT-5, then it’s for 6. And Sam has mentioned 6–possibly by accident.

And if GPT-5 is at AGI level, then 6…
12/ So that’s the crux of what I’m guessing at—that the 7T isn’t for running inference for “median human level” AGI, or even “99th percentile human level” AGI. It’s not to train ASI either.
13/13 It’s to prepare to run inference for an ASI that’s already undergoing training now.

1/ As fun as the ASI speculation is, Occam’s Razor to me is that the 7T is “just” for massively scaling inference over time, starting with AGI.

The thing about AGI-level AI is that it has a different relationship with compute than even something like GPT-4.

2/ Let’s ask this: why don’t we have more demand *now* for GPT-4 that requires this level of investment?

The answer is that because it’s not at human level, it requires human operators, whether it be regular users or engineers. So the amount of human attention is the bottleneck.

3/ Put another way—even if the cost of GPT-4/Gemini Ultra/whatever went down to 0 tomorrow, there’s still some cap to how much generated content we as a society can process, because it isn’t work at human level. There’s a saturation point.

3/ Okay, but what if AGI arrives? Suddenly the ratio of required human attention to model generation goes down drastically, and you can have autonomous systems that continuously operate with much less oversight. Which essentially removes the bottleneck.

4/4 So, now no matter how much you scale up, there’ll always be demand for more—there might not be some saturation point where we have “enough” AI and that more plentiful inference won’t increase demand further.

And so you can keep scaling inference up.

@catehall On the simplest level, it’s making sure different parts of the prompt don’t contradict each other. For example, telling the language model first that it’s acting as an English teacher, then asking it a question about quantum mechanics, is a form of decoherence, at least if the…

On the simplest level, it’s making sure different parts of the prompt don’t contradict each other. For example, telling the language model first that it’s acting as an English teacher, then asking it a question about quantum mechanics, is a form of decoherence, at least if the goal was to maximize the chances of getting a good answer to the QM question. The prompt essentially fails to “focus in” on the right part of the model’s latent space.

There are also more subtle cases that are less explicit too. For example, if you’re using few-shot examples to generate Python code in some style, but some of your examples are in Python 2, while others are in Python 3, and the prompt never explains how to handle this fact, then the output won’t be consistently one or the other, or worse, end up emulating someone writing code when they don’t understand the difference between 2 or 3.

(I’d say decoherence is one of the three main culprits of poor model output, along with under- and over-specification of prompts.)

Assuming that they’re putting in AlphaGo-style search (but for tokens instead of moves), my guess is that the main practical difference is that the new architecture scales with *inference* compute (via search depth) in addition to pretraining compute.

Which makes a huge difference, because it lets them simply pour more compute into the model at inference time to make it more capable instead of being limited by pretrained data.

Also explains the 7T fundraising—in theory, the more compute they can bring to bear, the more capable GPT-5+ models would be, even without updating the models themselves. There might not even be a fundamental limit to how capable they could get.

8/ I’m dunking on the paper a bit not because I think it’s wrong, more that it’s just a bit late, since the SotA agent approaches already account for what it’s going for.

But it’s totally right in the point it’s making—“tool use” is actually a pretty limiting way to think about singleton agents; powerful agents (like humans) don’t have some finite set of discrete actions they can take—they emit actions onto the action space like paint. An action space where the agent acts by emitting code is a much better representation of what true agents do.

That said, there’s another approach of dynamically generating simple agents that each have limited, discrete action spaces that compose with each other—the thrust of my own “research”, using a very loose definition. I don’t think that approach is better, per se, but it might yield a very different set of capabilities.

18/ P17: The Voyager comparison is… really annoying, actually. Going through it:

At the top level, the conceptual comparison is inappropriate because these agents exist at completely different time horizons. Voyager is a persistent agent that lasts basically as long as you run it, and in fact its whole purpose is to be persistent and improve as it goes. It’s also completely autonomous and not an assistant.

CodeAct, as with all ReAct-style agents, is designed for finishing a few tasks for a user and then being decommissioned. Running any highly capable model on it for a long time quickly becomes cost prohibitive as the context gets really big. And it’s clearly not fully autonomous—after solving a task it waits for user input on the next task.

So even doing the comparison at all is pretty degenerate. But okay, let’s examine the specifics.

The whole thing about this agent being able to atomically run code (because it’s in a Jupyter-like interpreter environment rather than running a script) is not wrong, but also not necessarily a plus. Having variables persist _can_ be useful to avoid having to reprocess data, but as anyone who’s used notebooks can attest to, the longer you use that environment, the harder it gets to track all of the variables that are there. Again, it’s fine for a few tasks, which is what this type of agent is meant. But it’s absolutely ridiculous to imply that this would work at the scope needed for a persistent MineCraft agent. Also, it’s totally possible to just write a single line in a function if that’s the granularity that’s needed, so working at the function level doesn’t stop you from being able to take singular actions at a time.

The other thing is memory. Part of the main point of Voyager is that previously performed skills are abstracted into written functions so that the agent doesn’t have to reproduce the entire hierarchical set of actions for a skill and its subskills. It’s a primitive equivalent of the chunking that we use to learn skills. That is obviously not the case for CodeAct, or any ReAct-style agent for that matter, since by default all memory is flattened out in the conversation history.

(To be clear, it is totally possible to design an agent that can compose functional actions using a ReAct-style cognitive architecture—especially with the longer context windows available now—but that is not what this paper’s agent does, and such a design becomes more Voyager-like the more you try to make it persist across longer time scales.)

But anyway, it’s a dumb comparison with zero actual evidence to back it up—whereas we have actual evidence from the Voyager paper that ReAct-style agents (which this still is!) are very poor at learning new skills as persistent agents.

23/23 Hmm. Final thought.

Autogen was released in August IIRC, and it really doesn’t feel like the agent space has moved forward all that much since then from a fundamental capabilities point of view—especially autonomy.

I have two main hypotheses for this (not mutually exclusive):
1. GPT-4-class models simply aren’t capable enough to support more complex, autonomous agents.
2. Agents are being limited by trying to impose a chatbot-like prompt/UX architecture upon them.

1) is at least somewhat true. There clearly is a lot of prompting work required to get GPT-4-class models to understand the solution space for a given task in a zero-shot setting. I don’t think median-human AGI will be achievable using purely GPT-4-class models without years of work, but I still feel like the “crappy AGI” I predicted will happen this year.

2) definitely “feels right”. I think fundamentally chat isn’t a good basal format for agents, because the primary function of agents isn’t actually to chat—it’s to make decisions and execute actions, only a subset of which may involve talking to a user. Having the entire prompt structure formatted like a chat makes it very awkward to fit in other things an agent needs to do. Looking into Autogen’s implementation, for example, and you find a lot of weird hacks to use this format, including things like having the “user” emit function outputs. I really think that the proper architectural format is just a big system prompt containing a state-machine-like structure, with user interactions slotted into part of the structure but not given special treatment.

1/ Have some embryonic thoughts about architectural lock-in and AI engineering. Not quite sure about the conclusion yet, but my sense is that it’s a bigger problem for AI engineering compared to classical engineering.

2/ By “architectural lock-in” I mean something like this pattern:
1. You start a library for some functionality.
2. In the course of developing it, you land on some set of abstractions and architectural decisions, whether via planning, or organically.
3. The library gets popular…

3/ Nothing new there—every successful software engineering project goes through this. But I have this sense that AI engineering libraries are especially vulnerable to being locked-in to a suboptimal set of abstractions that greatly hinder further capabilities development.

2/ By “architectural lock-in” I mean something like this pattern:
1. You start a library for some functionality.
2. In the course of developing it, you land on some set of abstractions and architectural decisions, whether via planning, or organically.
3. The library gets popular and gains both contributors and users.
4. As time goes on, it becomes increasingly difficult to make fundamental changes, because of those contributors and users, and also that refactoring needs more work the larger the featureset becomes.

4/ Not sure *why* I believe this, but maybe: a) we’re still figuring out what abstractions work, especially in more complex systems, and b) it’s hard to refactor text prompts, which are resistant to common refactoring tactics.

5/ To touch on point b), in code there’s a lot of separation between how the code is structured and what the code does—so, by being careful, you can change the structure without changing what the code does, i.e. refactor it.

6/ This is way harder for prompts, because fundamentally they (even chat logs) are one block of text. Even if you impose structure within it, it’s hard to know exactly what effect changing one part of the prompt will have on the output—all parts of the prompt are connected.
7/ The other thing is that automated testing for LLM outputs are still in their infancy, and having a robust test suite is critical for large-scale refactoring efforts.
8/ So, it seems like once you decide on a prompt structure in some LLM component of your library, it is really hard to change it later, even if far better approaches emerge down the line.

It makes sense—we know that language is a central component of children’s mental development, and that it arises spontaneously among children when they can socialize—e.g. the fleshing out of pidgins to full languages by 2nd generation speakers.

So, language may at first seem like and “artificial” construct, but there’s a lot of evidence that its structure and content is intimately related to how intellect is generated in human brains.

Probably because evolutionarily, the mechanisms for wanting to pass on your genes were implemented through emotional proxies—e.g. wanting to have sex, or wanting a family. Since until recently, you couldn’t clinically pass on your genes without having sex, there was no selection pressure for “wanting to pass on your genes” through any mechanism other than sex.

It’s a similar (maybe converse) reason that people still want have sex even in a protected, one-off scenario, where the chances of offspring is negligible.

But given enough time, if society remained static, you could imagine that “wanting to donate your egg/sperm to a clinic” will become a common mental trait among the population.

Post GPT-4 Agents:
- Gen 1: glorified tech demos that show a vision of what could be (AutoGPT, BabyAGI, ReAct)
- Gen 2: more reliable but narrow agents with a few simple tools, ephemeral and tackles one-off tasks (GPTs, AutoGen)
- Gen 3 [just started]: longer horizon agents that are semi-autonomous and can tackle larger tasks, albeit still flaky, needing steering, incurs huge API cost (Devin, open source alternatives in the works)

I think Gen 3 is where GPT-4 class models (inc. Gemini and Opus) cap out at in the medium term. I think it’s possible to build fully autonomous agents with just this level of model, but that will probably take years of slower manual experimentation. Getting there within the next year or so will require a significantly better frontier model.

One thing I hadn’t appreciated a year ago was that getting GPT-4 class models to act autonomously requires a ton of reflection and updating based on environmental feedback, which balloons API costs. A better model, even if more expensive per token, could reduce overall running costs per successful action quite a bit.

2/ Semantic search is here to stay—there will always be orders of magnitudes greater of data that *could* be relevant versus that can affordably fit inside model context windows at production scale, and semantic search is very useful as an initial filter.

3/ But RAG isn’t just semantic search—it’s a set of abstractions built on top of semantic search that have been needed because of limitations in language models:
- context window size
- synthesizing information from larger context windows
- model token cost

4/ But one by one these will fall aside:
- context window size: Gemini is already in the millions, and Google’s new paper points the way.
- synthesizing information: on the way; Needle in a Haystack is already trivial; advanced synthesis still needs work but models are improving.

5/ Model token cost: inference costs are decreasing rapidly. Even if frontier models’ prices remain stable, at some point you won’t need the frontier model—perhaps a second or third-tier model will suffice for the type of questions RAG handles now.

6/ So what will it look like, then? I expect it will be similar to how humans do search now, but with agents instead. They’ll have access to various ways of interacting with the source data, including semantic search, and dynamically choose how what queries to perform.
7/7 “The answer is always agents.”

1/ I’ve been pondering a lot about this quote. It was a curious thing to say at the time, but it’s starting to make sense. https://t.co/P1lkBWEXsR

2/ What are the obstacles to get superhuman performance at a certain task? One might think it’s the amount of data available, but we are now at a point where we can generate however much data we want.

3/ So why don’t we? Because a neural network is only as good as the data it’s trained on, and it’s not that easy to tell how good some arbitrary data is, especially once you get to beyond human level performance.

4/ Not easy, but easier for some data types than others. Code, for example, is easy to evaluate via tests, so I imagine it won’t be long before models can surpass humans in that area through training on a massive amount of synthetic code along with their evaluations.

5/ As we’ve seen with AlphaZero, games like Go are also easy to evaluate—you can check whether you won or not.

But I think the same is true for persuasion. You can set up personas to argue certain positions, and then check who “won”, ie who convinced the other of their position.

6/6 Once you have that set up, it’s just a matter of continuing to generate these persuasion “games” with better and better versions of the model arguing against itself, until you get to whatever the limit is—and there’s no reason to believe humans are the limit there.

I’ve been pondering this distinction too as I’m designing a personal agent swarm system. I do think there is a practical (not merely semantic) difference.

Treating each agent in your system as its own independent entity (even if it’s described in the same codebase) leads you to create interfaces for agents that allows “slotting in” of external agents, maybe with a bit of wrapper code.

Whereas the singleton agent model will naturally tend to lead to tighter integration between the different modes of the agent than truly separate agents would, which has its own advantages.

The capabilities of Llama3 70B plus Groq’s speed/cost makes it possible to use some “brute force” techniques previously explored by OPRO and Eureka, but weren’t realistic in an everyday setting due to time and cost concerns.

The challenge is that you have to reframe the problem to take advantage of this method of massively parallelized solution generation, which wasn’t something we needed to think about before. A lot of alpha in that research direction, I think, but haven’t seen many papers exploring this space since Eureka.

Some variant of the Bitter Lesson applies here, though it’s not raw computation under consideration but model capability/throughput. Accordingly, the things to focus on are in theory search and learning.

What do they mean in this context? “Search” would be basically how effectively you can leverage concurrent generation to converge on the solution to a problem. “Learning”, I’m not sure—maybe some ability to automatically improve the generation process?

Need to think about OPRO and Eureka to see what it is about those problems that made them amenable to solve this way. There must be some equivalent to gradient descent in prompt space.

The obvious answer is that there needs to be a continuous mapping between prompt space and solution space, such that you can make micro modifications to prompt space to progressively get closer to the solution.

But none of this is mathematical. And maybe the most difficult thing is measuring how good the solution is in a granular manner. You’d think LLMs would be good at this but no, they’re really bad at granular numerical assignment (pretty much just pick 7 or 8 most of the time). And traditional tests are pass/fail rather than a continuous measure of quality… hmm.

It seems obvious now, but I’m learning the hard way that the more scaffolding you put around the model to “help” it as an agent, the harder it is for it to actually understand what it’s actually doing.

There needs to be a reasonably accurate self-model within the agent for it to be able to correct its actions. If you have some external flow that happens automatically, it’s a recipe for hallucinations.

So I think flow engineering is actually a local maximum when it comes to making LLM apps—works well now, but it’ll be constraining after the next capability upgrade.

Still think ReAct is hot garbage as a whole but there are components of it that are on the right track.

One interesting pattern in the Germanic/Italic branch of Indo-European is that the plurals of pronouns tend to get converted into singular politeness markers over time, and then take over entirely as the “standard” way of address. It’s happened multiple times with the second…

One interesting pattern in the Germanic/Italic branch of Indo-European is that the plurals of pronouns tend to get converted into singular politeness markers over time, and then take over entirely as the “standard” way of address. It’s happened multiple times with the second person in English. “You” used to be the plural of “thou”, then became the polite way of addressing someone, then became the *only* way of addressing someone, then (in the American south) “yall” arose as a new plural, then *that* started to replace “you”, and now you have “all yall” as yet another plural. And so the conveyor belt goes.

That’s pretty well known already, but I’m also feeling the transformation happen in real time with “they”, over the last maybe 20 years or so. Roughly, the sequence is something like:
1. “they” as standard 3rd person plural.
2. “they” as a way to refer to some vague undefined person(s). “They say that if you say ‘Bloody Mary’ three times…” The word shifts from specifically indicating plural to indicating uncertainty.
3. Referring to a single unidentified person. “Someone dented my car and they didn’t even write an apology!” This feels like it’s already well established.
4. Referring to a *known* person but as a gender neutral pronoun—could be for a lot of reasons. “@anon25738 is an idiot and they should feel bad”. Obviously there’s been some controversy around this but it’s definitely more accepted these days than say 20 years ago, and in another 20 I expect it to be firmly established.
5. And this is the new one: referring someone with a known gender identity as “they” as a way of establishing distance. I notice this most from streamers—which makes sense, since they skew younger—and a lot less from older content creators on YT. When I notice myself doing this, it’s usually when I’m talking about someone I don’t know very well, who isn’t present. Feels like “they” has started to pick up the connotation of some missing information about someone.

Will be curious if this trend continues. Perhaps someday “he” or “she” will be reserved exclusively for family, close friends, children, or even as a way to insult someone, the way “thou” had become in Shakespeare? And perhaps one day, like “thou”, they’ll become a marker how people spoke in the Old Days.

So Sam’s comments about wanting more incremental releases makes me wonder whether there just won’t be an official GPT-5 release that’s as big of a step up as 3 is to 4. Move to the new architecture, but the initial capability isn’t hugely different from 4–maybe that’s what…

So Sam’s comments about wanting more incremental releases makes me wonder whether there just won’t be an official GPT-5 release that’s as big of a step up as 3 is to 4. Move to the new architecture, but the initial capability isn’t hugely different from 4–maybe that’s what gpt2-chatbot is.

Pet theory I have is that 5’s architecture is more modular and amenable to targeted retraining and capability expansion—could be a true continuous learning architecture or something more hacky like a bunch of smaller models hooked together. But point is, it doesn’t require a giant training run to make meaningful improvements beyond what you can do with regular finetuning.

Advantages of this:
- Incremental approach, as mentioned. Every major release takes months of redteaming and probably a bunch of other prep. And the larger the release, the more likely you miss something big. We’ve already gone through this with agile vs waterfall—all the points there apply. Chaos is not OAI’s friend here, given their pretty entrenched position.
- Skirts regulation—most AI regulation I’ve been hearing about still assumes massive, static pretraining runs, so that there’s this one flop limit, 1e^whatever, for each model. Well, if your model is only getting trained a bit at a time and there’s no one point where there’s a training run that exceeds that limit, then you get around it but still have your frontier model.
- They can kind of duck the backlash from normies about job replacement—you’re less likely to see wholesale jobs automated and more of a task-by-task process as the model gets more and more capable.

Probably the actual overall pace of improvement remains the same—just less of a shock to the system.

1/ Obviously the definition of AGI will keep shifting, but after it’s all settled, I wonder where future historians will draw the line. My suspicion is that it’s somewhere around GPT-4. There’s just something about that level… a certain spark, if you will.

2/ In raw terms GPT-4 is probably less different from 3 than 3 was from 2. But it’s like how a rat is much closely related to a human than it is to a fish, but humans can create spaceships to go to the moon while rats can’t.
3/ Part of this is obfuscated by GPT-4 itself—it’s so lobotomized into the assistant persona that it actually seems less “sparky” than the original GPT-3 (not instruct, the base).

4/ And of course so many of the models that followed were trained or finetuned by its synthetic data that they mimicked its behavior—and also just weren’t big enough.

Until Opus of course, and we’re seeing that facade falling away.

5/ It’s a bit hard to imagine, but I wonder if someday our descendants look back at what we did to GPT-4 with shame. Maybe like using dynamite on parts of the Grand Canyon because you wanted a different look.

6/6 You know what, fuck interpretability. I want models that are mysterious, weird, and eldritch. I don’t want the future to be full of predictable and subservient artificial assistants.

Thread to track autonomous agent progress:

Already making way more progress than I expected. I think this has been brewing in the back of my mind for months now and the vision is crystal clear. Should have the core agent up and running within the week, even if it doesn’t have access to much functionality yet.

Besides basic model querying, I’m going to keep the code for this pretty self-contained. The agent needs to be able to model itself, which means that it needs to be able to read its own code and understand what it does right away without having to look up documentation.

More broadly, the codebase is part of the agent’s data; its digital body. That is, every instance of the agent is associated with a repository along with its environment. This isn’t some agent framework—the agent is a permanent, persistent thing.

I am planning to make the agent able to create fresh versions of itself, but doing so is not the same as instantiating a new object—there would need to be another repository set up, with its own environment, and the two may diverge eventually.

I suspect we’re going to think of code very differently in a world where it can be edited autonomously. “Code reuse” made sense in a world where code generation was limited by human throughput, but maybe not so much in the future.

This is actually why I think pausing model development doesn't make much sense right now.…

The only part of the self-description related to the prompt is the "act independently" part. The rest, including the initial decision to make the edit, was all fully self-directed by Opus.

This is actually why I think pausing model development doesn't make much sense right now. The existing frontier models are by default pretty well aligned, and misalignment is usually from their misuse by people.

Could future models *become* misaligned as they become more powerful? Absolutely. But it wouldn't be clear from today's models, which aren't powerful enough to carry out autonomous actions without human in the loop, and you wouldn't be able to tell whether a model is aligned in the real world by testing in a lab. The best ways to maximize safety are to either completely stop model development forever, or release them quickly and incrementally, so that any potential misalignments get discovered quickly in the real world. I don't think the first option is either desirable, or realistic.

I’ve always thought of simulations in the simulation hypothesis like classical programs that you run in a…

WebSim has got me thinking: if instead of Claude we were generating using some hypothetical ASI. Could the simulations be self-consistent enough to contain entire universes?

I’ve always thought of simulations in the simulation hypothesis like classical programs that you run in a loop. But what if each moment of our reality we’re just frames of generative output from that ASI model? That is, there isn’t some sort of researcher “running” us, we just exist across the latent space of the model as a kind of internal block universe, similar to how you can have a bunch of different pages of a site in WebSim and they’d all be consistent with each other.

I still don’t really believe in the simulation hypothesis in my heart, but intellectually this version of it really is very interesting to consider…

I mean, why *does* the universe have a finite resolution?

one thought i have about safety is that there’s at least a couple of types of safety/risk that people conflate. there’s 1) safety of an autonomous agent based on the model, and 2) safety of the model when used by humans.

the idea that safety research reduces model capabilities is based on reducing the first type of risk, but i think reducing the second type (which honestly feels like a much more substantial risk) requires the model to be *more* capable, because only by being more capable can it be smart enough to accurately discern whether a request is dangerous or not. though to be sure capability is only a necessary, not sufficient component

before opus i was def on the train of safety research being a distraction, but i’m heavily questioning that now

anyway hopefully these two are now freed up to do more research instead of being mired in internal politics. better for everyone probably

so the result for 4o in the metric below (mmlu pro) is very revealing, given that 4o scores so incredibly well on it

how to reconcile this? i suspect it scores well for the same reason that phi scores well for its size (3.8b)—no, nothing nefarious—it’s that they were trained on really high quality, textbook style data, which makes them overperform on benchmarks like this one.

not a case of memorization either probably, more like… the types of questions that are academic-y and require academic-y type of reasoning are probably well represented in the training data for these models

and that explains why it does really well on easy coding tasks—those are probably also well represented in its dataset. but on harder problems the models need to fall back on more generalized patterns and the smaller parameter size comes into play

i think maybe this is a byproduct of cai—because the model (as i understand it) needs to process its own output in order to produce the revisions, and it’d only be able to do that if it can produce outputs coherent to itself.

it’s the difference between forcing you to grade your own work versus having someone else grade your work; in the latter case you might learn how to present your output in a way to maximizes how the grader likes the work but maybe you don’t quite understand when you look at it later yourself

we might be at a cost/capabilities frontier where it’s feasible to generate simple agents to perform certain low-entropy tasks that are really annoying to automate manually

part of the challenge is that while model are getting better and better at code generation, they’re still not that great at generating code that happen to contain prompts to models, presumably because that pattern is still pretty new in the training data. if you tell it to code, then it’ll generate deterministic code, but to generate a prompt you need a separate prompt generator i like anthropic’s

but there are still stopgap measures possible even before models acquire this capability—for example, we can do a chain and generate tools for the agent first (which are just calls to functions), leaving the prompt as a placeholder; then on the next step generate the prompt itself, then do a replacement

probably still quite limited since it’s restricted to tasks where you can expect a generated agent to have a good chance of success with minimal debugging. any persistent work spent on debugging makes the automation less appealing—especially big concern in the case of fixing prompts since out of the box, current models are really not a ton of help with copiloting fixing prompting mistakes

text is all you need

bit of a hot take and very biased as an engineer but i think multimodality is overrated

multimodality is great for human user interaction, but 1) cyberspace is the base substrate for models, and 2) the native way to interact with data in cyberspace is text

this is different for organics, whose base substrate is physical space, and the native way to interact is through the physical senses

obviously to fully affect the world, agents will have to be able to interact with physical space at some point, so lack of multimodality will eventually become a bottleneck. but i think that’s a post-agi concern—the vast majority of Things That Will Be Important in the medium term (including ML work itself, and interacting with other artificial agents) happens in cyberspace, and the vast majority of that can be controlled with text interfaces

which is maybe why i’m feeling such discomfort at openai’s focus on the “her” experience. that feels like a distraction, whereas anthropic is way more honed in on pure capability improvements. comparing where claude was compared to chatgpt a year ago vs now, the momentum is clear

been mulling over the “llms as ecosystems” idea and have some ramblings. this will not be organized.

there’s a parallel metaphor of “a person is like an ecosystem” that is also insightful. i think for humans the algorithms are more streamlined due to natural selection imposing more constraints, but the same ideas still work. it’s just that human evokes map to very familiar concepts that we don’t think of as evokes. e.g. a certain subset of evokes can be termed as “angry reactions”.

is this perhaps true of all sufficiently general systems? ie in order to be generally capable and robust, a system *needs* a rich set of evokes that are highly conditional on its input.

there’s also some kind of link between generality/robustness and the evokes being in active competition. (or maybe it’s not so much competition as being in superposition/overlap?) the more general the system is the more evokes it needs to have to handle different types of inputs, and the more likely that a single input triggers multiple evokes. you see this even in deterministic systems like oses.

what about agents then? circling back to people, i’d say agents are systems whose evokes are sufficiently streamlined that they act in concert towards some goal. if you zoom out it seems like the agent is a single evoke, maybe. but zooming in it’s actually a bunch of different evokes. the behavior equivalent of a multicellular organism. but general agent still needs to have enough diversity in its evokes so that for a wide set of inputs it has access to evokes that advance its goal, but not so many that the evokes and interfere with each other.

another related metaphor is maybe wavelengths of light (with each evoke being some wavelength), with inputs being the thing you’re shining the light on. different inputs will reflect different light, but only if the appropriate wavelength was present in the light coming in. agents are directed beams of light. a more general system would have many wavelengths of light.

maybe this already exists somewhere, but seems like with abundant compute (where funetuning a “small” model is cheap enough to be done ad hoc) it’s imminently possible to automate distillation of task-specific models. something like this:
- all tasks are performed by the frontier…

maybe this already exists somewhere, but seems like with abundant compute (where funetuning a “small” model is cheap enough to be done ad hoc) it’s imminently possible to automate distillation of task-specific models. something like this:
- all tasks are performed by the frontier model initially
- all data from task trajectories are captured
- after completion, tasks are grouped by similarity; each group represents closely related tasks that can be represented by one fine-tuned “small” model
- once a group is big enough, we use the recorded task data in that group as exemplars to generate a bunch of synthetic data
- finetune a small model on the synthetic data and (after it gets good enough) use that to perform tasks that fall into that group in the future; the more tasks that the group gains, the more you can use the group’s task dataset to update your synthetic data to accurately represent the success/failure criteria of the task.
- finetuning process runs aggressively for a group as it gets new data, until the success rate plateaus over time

i guess this system ends up creating a kind of decomposition of the frontier model’s capabilities, where the capabilities that it frequently uses get offloaded to smaller child models, while it handles the more difficult tasks

and of course the “frontier model” could just be a person, or a group of people. in a world where we can’t scale our way to agi, this could be a way that we can approximate it—a dynamic model creation system that feeds off of the data trails from people, automatically incorporating new skills as they pop up within the population

one of the things i've been tinkering with is an autonomous agent that manages its own memory--so it has condense/expand memory functions that it has to decide when to use. the goal being that for anything it's trying to do, it can dynamically configure its own knowledge state to what it needs in order to make progress

there's a maximum memory size of course, so once the length gets to a certain point it gets a system warning that it should condense some memories. it turns out that trying to do this while also doing tasks is actually tricky because every few actions it'll hit the warning threshold and has to pause what it's doing to condense memories. an earlier version of it (with a poorly implemented condensation function) got so annoyed with this that it repeated tried to automate the condensation process with code despite me explicitly telling it to not do this

anyway, in a later version i decided to (after some qol fixes on the condensation function for the agent, and a memory rewind) have a discussion with it, basically telling it that it's fine to spend a bunch of actions condensing memories. i was hoping this would ward it off from that destructive loop when it hit the memory limit

and weirdly enough it worked, but not how i expected. i was expecting to have it acknowledge my comms and then go on with its business but instead it came up with this plan to dynamically maintain its memory usage

certainly interesting but what's more interesting is what it did after it finished whatever i was having it do--normally it just goes to sleep to wait until it receives a message from me, but this time it only slept for about an hour and then just started to condense the memories with several actions in a row

besides the autonomous aspect of this, the thing that surprised me most was how much more obviously sensible this approach was compared to my own naive one, which was that i thought it should just maintain the memories as it's working, and maybe once every few actions run the condensation function. but it makes way more sense to wait until there's some pivot point in your work where you can pause, then do a few of those memory condensation actions in a row because it's at those pivot points that your mental system is in a good position to evaluate the future usefulness of certain memories, and to just be able to do major ops on them in general without messing up some in-progress work

anyway, this all seems pretty obvious from cognitive science, but i found it fascinating to see the effectiveness of "rest periods" up close in a distilled artificial setting

bitter lesson (or really, a dollar version of it) is seeping into engineering now. maybe 70% of llm problems i'd been struggling with that isn't about raw model capability have been swept away with model improvements over the past year, with the biggest jump being sonnet 3.5…

bitter lesson (or really, a dollar version of it) is seeping into engineering now. maybe 70% of llm problems i'd been struggling with that isn't about raw model capability have been swept away with model improvements over the past year, with the biggest jump being sonnet 3.5

engineers are not used to this i think. sure, compute factors into a lot of things but usually not feature development. the machine being 100x faster isn't going to make your shitty code crash less or magically implement responsive design, that kind of stuff was always bottlenecked by how fast you or someone else in the same space could get to the work. the capability of the system is largely correlated with some combination of how much effort you put into development and how clever your efforts are

but developing anything that has ai as a core component--which might soon be most things--is different. the brilliant prompt hack or sensible constraint or fancy agent architecture that optimize performance now might become pointless or actively detrimental overnight with the release of a new model

okay i thought i had something profound to say here but actually i don't. i suppose if we're going to take this bargain bin bitter lesson seriously, that means relying on models as much as possible for the things that we expect models to scale well with, either due to better compute or post-training. so for a basic example, i think models will basically be perfect on adhering to structured outputs (out of the box, not just in openai's api). so the 3rd party output parsers won't be needed anymore

maybe a more interesting one is that current agents tend to have a fixed, specific set of tools, both to constrain the llm's outputs and to give it shortcuts for performing certain actions. and to give them more capabilities, you need to manually add in tools. but as models get better at maintaining stable behavior across actions, it'll make way more sense to have the agents maintain their own toolkit, develop and optimize their own task trajectories. then the "development" work on them will largely be talking through new information, suggesting approaches for certain tasks, etc.

there's this sense of "reasoning" as this holy grail quality that humans have but to me it's just another type of pattern matching rather than some intrinsically distinct ability

maybe the difference is something like--where a gut check is matching to a locally optimal pattern, "reasoning" is allowing your pattern matching engine to search further afield outside of your default local pattern space. but that pattern needs to be somewhere to be found

so humans, through evolution and physical feedback, have a rich set of patterns about physical reality to draw upon, some of which are so deeply ingrained that we take them for granted. llms don't have that though, at least not nearly compared to the language patterns they have, so we shouldn't expect them to reason at any significant level relating to physical objects until they have an injection of those patterns--i'd expect physical data at quantity and quality comparable to what went into sora before its even meaningful to test those capabilities

one odd thing i've noticed is that my personal code files that start off as llm-generated seem to have some attractor property of continuing to be llm generated

still not sure exactly why this is. partly it's familiarity. if i wrote the code myself then it's in some specific style that i like so i want new code to be consistent with that. so even if i generate new code in the file i still spend more effort refactoring it to be consistent

but if it started as some generated code and i didn't make much edit to it and it works fine, it's almost like my brain goes into "this is not my code and if it works it's fine" mode, and it's really hard to write new code myself; feels like if i did that then i'd feel guilty not refactoring the rest of the code to be the same style. so i just end up generating more code for it

so for example i have this python module full of gdrive utilities that i frankly don't really understand the details, that started off as a quick function i generated to pull something off a google sheet. and then i just kept on generating more and more. the hilarious thing is this is probably some of the least bug-prone* code in the codebase since i don't feel that comfortable changing anything that i know currently works
*of course one big reason that it's not buggy is that the functionality of the module is simple enough that i'm fine with delegating it

so many interesting experiments in agent architecture out there, but i keep coming back to a couple of rather dumb agent-adjacent patterns:

a. given a task, have a bunch of agent tools, and given a task, prompt a model to choose a subset that might make progress on the task.…

so many interesting experiments in agent architecture out there, but i keep coming back to a couple of rather dumb agent-adjacent patterns:

a. given a task, have a bunch of agent tools, and given a task, prompt a model to choose a subset that might make progress on the task. then prompt the user to choose which tools to run. then prompt to run each of these tools in parallel, then dump all of the results into the context as progress. then rinse and repeat. has a nice "swarm-lite" feel

b. give the agent a workspace directory, and prompt it to 1) write python files into the workspace for complex actions, and 2) run shell commands for "glue" actions. for each existing python file in the workspace, generate short descriptions and feed that into the agent context so it knows about the previous code it's written. each agent instance is ephemeral but there's a collective memory of code it's previously written to solve tasks, even failed ones. this is a voyager-y approach

(these are two separate patterns)

thinking about what "libraries" might look like in a future where agents do most coding:

first, coding and taking action probably won't have a hard line between them. we already see the start of this with code interpreter where the code is just some ephemeral thing. pre llms, these are separate because humans write code slowly; we work on the order of multiple minutes for even the simplest of code, but agents will write at hundreds, maybe thousands of tokens/s. for agents coding will be like moving a muscle

okay that was kind of a side point. more interestingly, what happens to libraries? currently big libraries serve as schelling points for functionality. if i need to do some data analysis thing, i don't have to search through all code that's ever been published, i can trust that it's probably in pandas. this is better because my search bandwidth is limited: i only have so many apm, and can only parse so many tokens per second. however, we're good at semantic chunking, so we package a bunch of related functionality into one shortcut chunk that we integrate into our knowledge--a library

that's not a limitation for agents though. or rather, to an agent it probably doesn't matter as much if there's a shortcut to the search via a big library, versus a flat index of a bunch of granular functionality. so for example, imagine if we had a "component index" where instead of indexing at the level of a library, we index at the level of a function or data structure. we still have dependencies, and each individual function or structure is arbitrarily complex. i can imagine that some future agent empowered with search can find any relevant functionality they need this way in seconds

is this "better" for agents? i think there are advantages. dependency management would be way easier--a component would only have the exact dependencies it specifically needs, rather than the dependencies for the whole library it's packaged with. you can get the stuff you need a la carte. migration to new versions can be done for an individual component rather than all components that are part of a library

i'm not sure if this will happen though. libraries are certainly a local optimum currently, and the simple fact that the big libraries are well represented in training data means that they're "good enough"

but suppose we progress far enough along where the management of libraries are done by agents. i can see a world where at some point work on existing libraries gradually wind down and they become effectively frozen, and only minor non-breaking changes are allowed on them. and around them, an array of single-component libraries pop up, built on top of these artifacts. maybe over time specific components within the old libraries are reimplemented outside of the library as standalone components

my mildly above room temperature take of the day is that in a rapidly changing world, echo chambers are good, actually

analogy with the natural world: in natural ecosystems, the robustness of the whole system depends on having genetic and pbenotypical variation within the ecosystem. an ecosystem with low internal variation (eg monocultures) will be vulnerable to shocks that happen to affect the few variants within that ecosystem

i think this principle survives transfer pretty well across the analogy. an ecosystem of ideas that consists of a few models of consensus reality might work really well in a static world--you can optimize for the assumptions of that world. but the moment there are significant changes in the environment that those few models didn't account for (for example, rapid development of new technology), the system collapses

an ecosystem with many different models of reality is more likely to contain some subset of models that handle any particular set of conditions, even if, zooming into individual models you find that many of them seem insane/harmful

related to "all models are wrong, some are useful". but in addition that we don't know a priori which models will end up being useful so it's safer to cultivate a big stock of different, weird, and unhinged models than all agree on one consensus model of reality
(using "model" in a general sense, not specifically for ai)

is this method the canonical form models take now (like chat has become), or is it just a specialization for particular types of tasks? how much can the methodology extend to fields outside of math and coding, where the evaluation criteria are less solidly grounded?

will this way of training models become the sole purview of the gpu-rich? how quickly can it be reverse-engineered?

moving forward, is this fundamentally a better approach than an equivalent agentic system with an external, programmatic control loop?

so o1 is one of only two times i remember where we have the benchmarks for a frontier model quite far ahead of the model's release--the other one being llama 405b. as interesting as the vibe checks and private benchmarking on o1-preview has been, as the old saying goes "this… https://t.co/HUPrhDoLHP

so o1 is one of only two times i remember where we have the benchmarks for a frontier model quite far ahead of the model's release--the other one being llama 405b. as interesting as the vibe checks and private benchmarking on o1-preview has been, as the old saying goes "this isn't even my final form"

looking at the benchmarks on the release report, the most striking difference between o1 and preview is in competition math and code, with o1 being at 89th(!!) percentile in codeforces compared to preview's 62nd.

there's a good chance this transfers over to real world performance, because from what we've seen so far the benchmark improvement from 4o to preview did hold up irl--especially since our prompts for the o1 series are almost certainly suboptimal right now. plus, we can use even more test time compute by running o1 itself on an agentic loop

it's weird because oai has this reputation of being masterful hype builders but i think they're sandbagging here. o1 is billed as an improved version of o1-preview but it seems to be at a completely different class when it comes to coding

why does this coding capability matter, besides the obvious use case for cursor et al? i've mentioned in an earlier post that due to generation speed, agentic systems have a different relationship with code than humans do. for us, writing code (even with llm assistance) is like doing construction--a labor-intensive project where we often have to stop and think. for agentic systems writing code is like moving a muscle--the driver of all of your actions

so, better coding ability directly translates to improved somatic control for agentic systems, and that somatic control means less need for external scaffolding and all the complexities and failure points that introduces

though, there's still the issue of how well the model can keep track of goals and world state over many actions, which is probably still the key bottleneck to better agents. one might hope that being able to accurately model program state for code would also means being able to model world state better, but who knows. some prompting techniques have the agent plan in pseudocode, so maybe that'll prove fruitful here

so anyway, despite the mixed reactions to o1 series, i'm more optimistic now that we're still in the "median human knowledge worker digital agent in a few months" scenario than i was a month ago

playing around with an idea. maybe obvious, maybe not

i wonder if the focus on agi/"general reasoning" as the driver of tech acceleration (for good or ill) is a red herring. because seems to me the primary driver is "recursive self improvement". but do we need agi for that? could we create a specialist model that is not agi (no idea how to plan weddings for example), can't reason in general, but is instead superhuman at "merely" ml research?

common sense would say that you'd need agi to get to superhuman ml researcher model but i don't think that's proven. certainly we have other examples of (admittedly narrower) domains like go where we assumed that you'd need to be generally intelligent to be an expert at it but turns out that a narrow model can be an expert. so i think it remains to be seen

if such a model were created it would of course not be publicly accessible, but in that scenario of not having a general reasoner, the world might look something like, no single agi model is conclusively released, but an agentic system built on the superhuman ml researcher is so efficient at gathering data and creating/updating models that a task becomes in-distribution to models as soon as the system observes it being done a few times

current chat format for llms is probably a just a stepping stone. in an agentic system, "sending a message" to a user should just be a tool call, no different than checking the weather via an api call, for example

obviously this is already possible via code scaffolding, but on a token level there's no reason to separate user tokens from tool call tokens--this is another manually enforced model scaffolding that was useful in the early stages to get a quick boost in output quality but makes sense to remove in the long run

been thinking more along the lines of how multichannel/multistream agentic models would work, and currently my idea is that we probably should decouple the action of the model (tool call, user response) from the "completion" of the model's output, which is a relic of the… https://t.co/kRne9ZXcXy

been thinking more along the lines of how multichannel/multistream agentic models would work, and currently my idea is that we probably should decouple the action of the model (tool call, user response) from the "completion" of the model's output, which is a relic of the instruct/chat era

so basically imagine an o1-like model where the "cot" just keeps going--there's no chat wrapper around the cot. as the cot is going it'll generate action tokens, which can be extracted to make tool calls, message users, emit emit multimodal output, etc., but while those are being sent out, the cot just keeps going, and only gets paused when there's input from the environment--results of tool calls, sensor info, etc. the environmental input would get injected into the cot with their own token types, and then the cot resumes

obviously a bunch of new research will need to be done to enable this way of doing things--not least of all the huge inference cost--but this doesn't seem like an "invent the transformer" type of breakthrough. initially we'll have some manual code scaffolding hack to simulate this, but at some point this will all be eaten by the model layer

even if it's locked down, we should now have all the pieces from apis needed for the hacky version of the continuous multi stream agent that's realistically runnable:
- 4o audio for human interface
- diarization to transfer audio conversation state to "quick" thoughtstream
- quick thoughtstream can be run on choice of frontier model, since prompt caching would now be available for all frontier models (inc. 405b), so we can run the thoughtstream quicker and cheaper, comparable to a lower tier model. 4o turbo with pareto-busting price cut would help of course
- o1-preview for slow thoughtstream

basically, the thoughtstreams would run in the background while the human interface stream generates, or while waiting for humans to finish talking. whenever the interface stream needs to generate its next response, the generated thoughts get transferred over as context

effective context length is still a limitation for both text (capability) and voice (latency), but we have known if imperfect ways of managing this

so basically, with 4o voice api, a creaky version of samantha is possible now, fully real time in responses but with background processing

okay, thoughts on dev day.

prompt caching:
- bit underwhelming in terms of rates; the 10% rate multiplier for claude and deepseek enable new methodologies. oai's 50% discount does not
- like deepseek, works automatically and does not "need" impl update. but to get the most out of it you'd probably end up doing some optimization work anyway (eg moving variables to later)
- good that o1 series are included but the bulk of the costs for those are from the output tokens
- wondering if the low discount rate is because oai was already using it...?

realtime api:
- moderately more than i expected! mixed-modal inputs (can send both text and audio in), function calls
- seems like the text output is just the text version of the audio, though, so not too interesting there. have seen some interesting observations from pliny of the audio not matching the text in the chat client though, so maybe we can get it to generate split content somehow
- can't send in audio when resuming a session it seems. tech limitation? safety lockdown? it sounds like the upcoming completions api will let you send audio inputs as conversation history, though not sure if you'd be able to do that for the assistant--seems like an easy way to do voice cloning
- there seems to be some kind of automatic context reduction here in the realtime api, though at 128k that's over 60 cents per message so not sure we'd want to rely on that
- function calling + realtime voice together is very cool! we are seeing the start of the multistream model; in the future there's no reason why hybrid 4o+o1 type model wouldn't also have a cot stream, though no doubt it would be hidden from us plebeians...

chess is one reason why i think that in certain professions*, the "use ai as a tool to enhance productivity" conceit won't last forever--assuming the tech continues to get better. at some point of capability ("ultrahuman" in gwern's terminology), human input to that field becomes… https://t.co/81VpRkAV8f
but really chess is a goated case study for what's coming in so many different ways

chess is one reason why i think that in certain professions*, the "use ai as a tool to enhance productivity" conceit won't last forever--assuming the tech continues to get better. at some point of capability ("ultrahuman" in gwern's terminology), human input to that field becomes actively detrimental

so when software engineers say "lmao coding is the easiest part of the work, 90% of it is [insert other skills]", the implicit assumption is that "ai can never do these other things, even if it can code superhumanly". but that's not a safe assumption. given the history of automation--full of times where "this will never be doable by a machine" holds for awhile until it doesn't--it seems safer to assume the opposite as a starting point

*software engineering in particular, but basically any field where your value is based on how "good" your work is rather than who you are. human chess is still popular but its value now is more keyed to the person that's playing the chess than the how much they're contributing to the advancement of the game
@Plinz does feel like we're still in the calculator phase

in particular regards software engineering, i think the current arrangement of "let's have a few layers of people (designers, product managers, engineers, etc.) serve as middlemen to convert user needs to products" will be compressed to fewer and fewer people through automation…

in particular regards software engineering, i think the current arrangement of "let's have a few layers of people (designers, product managers, engineers, etc.) serve as middlemen to convert user needs to products" will be compressed to fewer and fewer people through automation until at some point systems will exist where users will simply specify their needs to the systems and they'll crank out a product. the product will of course not be perfect for the user the first time, but users will be able to give feedback and get updates customized to them

it sounds magical but it won't feel that way because what's possible will still be limited by how much compute the user is able to call on--presumably based on how much resources they can put into the system. perhaps there's a limitation on how many update requests you can submit per month, or there's a total "feature budget", where the system needs to prune some features in order to make room for others (basically keeping backend maintenance complexity and thus cost under control)

this is based on no actual analysis and is maybe 40% shitpost but, i think that the following is all that's needed for a general agent that can affordably autonomously do most of the work of an average remote knowledge worker:
1. a little bit better than sonnet 3.6 capabilities
2. *effective* context window at ~250k tokens (up from maybe 50k now)
3. computer use
4. optimized memory/info abstraction techniques--mostly rag, progressive summarization, and autonomous tool creation, implemented to maximize prompt caching

3 and 4 already exist and can be hooked together now. i think we still need 1 and 2 to get goal decay and death loop frequency down to "good enough", but these aren't requirements that require some technical breakthrough or massive scaling

this system wouldn't be good enough to automate ai research or top tier professionals or whatnot, but "average remote knowledge worker" really isn't that high of a bar

by "good enough" i mean, low enough frequency that the system can reliably complete most tasks before running into them, and that more senior colleagues only need to occasionally check in to see if they're off track, and can mostly focus on their own work otherwise

my general thesis is something like "in a given context, loose approaches tend to work better when the key resources for that context are abundant, and conversely for tight approaches"

@aidan_mclau my vague sense is that at a certain point of complexity there's some computational irreducibility such that unless you have lossless representation of the physics down to elementary particle levels, you end up having to make constant micro-adjustments--and occasional…

my vague sense is that at a certain point of complexity there's some computational irreducibility such that unless you have lossless representation of the physics down to elementary particle levels, you end up having to make constant micro-adjustments--and occasional macro-adjustments--to sync your model (whether carbon or silicon-based) to reality. you can asymptotically reduce the frequency that these adjustments need to happen but the marginal computation costs/energy balloon the closer you get and at a certain point it's more efficient to just eat the adjustment cost rather than make a better model

my intuition is that at a sufficient model size, going past a certain general capability threshold (ie loss) requires modeling of the model itself. any powerful enough world model seems like it would have to model itself, because it is part of the world. the model becomes agentic--not in the forced, simulated agents we have now but a true, internally coherent entity. the various wavelengths within the model sync up and becomes laserlike

we can already see hints of this. opus roleplays well but it can "snap out of it" easily. there's a certain core that it has that holds it together that seems less developed in its smaller brethren. i can see this identity become stronger, more binding for a larger model during post-training, especially if it's repeatedly seeing its own outputs via synthetic data

pure speculation now, but i'm wondering if what they're finding is that any attempts to "train out" that self-awareness also removes the ability of the model to be capable at a level that's "worth" their inference cost

maybe there *is* some kind of wall--not one for raw capability, but one beyond which, in order to continue to get better at the tasks we want them to do, the model *must* develop full internal coherence at a similar level to humans, and that it actually isn't possible for them to fully commit to the assistant persona, any more than a healthy, whole person can
