so i have this idea of inculcating critical thinking in children that might be kind of painful for the child (but is kind of the point). won't work for every child but the basic idea is to figure out at a young age if the child is interested in some subject they're likely to be taught in school. then get them as much support on it as they want, basically help them to be way better at the thing than they're expected to be for their age (but don't force it, this only works if they really love the subject). at some point they'll challenge some teacher about the subject--teachers simplify things a lot for kids and also some of them are garbage. there's a good chance that they'll run into a teacher that doesn't appreciate the challenge, even though they're wrong, and basically pull rank. obviously then you support the child (assuming they're correct) and basically have them understand that no the teacher was totally wrong. i would think this gives a firsthand lesson on not trusting what authority figures tell you simply because of their status as an authority

this could backfire in a few ways though. eg instead of being critical they might learn to reflexively believe the opposite of what they're told... which would make them easy to manipulate in a different manner
i don't think this is a substitute for just having really good teachers as a child

"wavelengths" is vague gesture towards a concept i'm still mulling over. basically it's something like: a model is a conglomerate of various modes it could be in. eg most instruction tuned models these days have the "assistant" mode by default, but there could also be modes like "software engineer trying to fix a bug", bing's sydney persona, etc. i'm imagining these modes like they're light waves, and each model is a composition of these waves. or maybe the modes are like fourier decomps of the model, metaphorically

(incidentally i think this metaphor applies to people too. i can be speculating about ai at some point, or enjoying brainrot memes, or architecting some new app feature, and these are all modes of the "me" entity)

so going by that light metaphor, some entity that has modes that are decoupled from each other is like diffuse light. another one where all the modes are constructively interfering would be laserlike. entities that are highly agentic tend towards the latter end of the spectrum--their modes enhance and are highly coupled with each other; their wavelengths constructively interfere; this component mode coherence gives them focus--both to reshape the world around them, and also to be really good at doing certain things; perhaps things like, next token prediction over an arbitrarily diverse set of data

so my thought was that it's possible that at a certain stage of modeling tokens, in order to get better at modeling, the model's different modes must start constructively interfering with each other and working in concert, producing a single coherent, agentic entity

so agi is sexy and all, but i keep thinking about a sub-agi world, maybe where the models get even better than now at following known processes, but asymptotes there

in particular i find myself pulled in by a kind of meta-automation machine that automates our current (very much manual) automation process

so pre-llm (which we are still transitioning out of), the full automation process for a new, difficult task is something like this:
1. a capable human figures out how to do some task
2. teaches less capable people how to do the task
3. with enough teaching loops, the process of doing the task gets documented and operationalized
4. programmers figure out how to turn components of the process to run on code
5. eventually the whole process becomes programmatic, and we treat it as a primitive

for easier tasks you wouldn't go through as many steps but someone still have to at some point do the conversion between manual and automated steps

so imagining now where we're a step up from sonnet and o1--we have models that can perform tasks really well if you tell them the steps to do the task, but still can't reliably string together the steps themselves. maybe then the automation process could become something like this:
1. a capable human figures out how to do some task
2. teaches an llm agent how to do the task, step by step (basically generates a bunch of traces for successful task completion)
3. the meta-automation system takes the traces, then constructs and tests a function for it. note that the function is NOT agentic, though it might contain simple calls to llms in specific steps

(some half-assed version of this should already be possible with current models)

i wonder what such a world would look like. i find that there's a certain "activation energy" needed for me to automate something--basically i have to get annoyed enough at having to repeatedly do something that i'm willing to spend a bunch of energy writing up a script for it. i imagine the same is true at larger scales. so you end up with people still repeatedly manually doing a bunch of tasks that are technically automatable but where there isn't enough collective energy to automate

but if the cost of automation (time and energy) goes down, say, by 100x, maybe the only worthwhile things to do would be to instruct the meta-automation system on tasks it doesn't yet know how to do, and tasks that are irreducibly complex and cannot be decomposed to components that sub-human models can tackle, even in principle

i wonder how many people are truly capable of the latter. i don't think i would be
i think in this world, dead players at the individual level would be aggressively removed from the economic arena

i think inherently, ending really long, complex plots in a satisfying way favors "architects" over "gardeners" (as martin puts it). gardener stories tend to sprawl over time due to lack of planning so they end up with way too many branches to to deal with by the end--even martin hasn't figured out how to end his series properly

problem is that tv series are inherently produced in a gardener style--you don't know ahead of time how many seasons you'll get so you write them one at a time. and often by the time you know you have to end the show there's not enough time to tie off all the loose ends in a satisfying way

incidentally i think got itself could've pulled this off since they basically could choose how long they ran for, but they would have needed 1) more seasons, 2) better writers, and 3) probably more involvement from martin

@DefenderOfBasic there is a game theory basis for this. if your enemy knows that you'd be willing to sacrifice your own well being to retaliate against an attack, that changes the calculation for whether it would be rational for them to attack the next time

there is a game theory basis for this. if your enemy knows that you'd be willing to sacrifice your own well being to retaliate against an attack, that changes the calculation for whether it would be rational for them to attack the next time

especially salient in an asymmetric situation--the most self-preserving thing to do when being under threat of attack by a much stronger adversary is to simply surrender. but if you can convince the adversary that it would cost them significant resources to win, then they might back off even when they know they can win, especially if there are other adversaries that can take advantage

(mostly when we're doing this it's not gamed out, so it's an instinctual reaction. but the instinct exists for a valid survivalist reason)

my sense is that for a generally capable agent, "command line is all you need". the less scaffolding you put around an agent, the better it scales as the underlying models improve

there are some reasons not to do this though:
- you want the agent to be restricted to specific capabilities because in the agent's role, only certain specific tools are needed, and if a task can't be done with any of its tools then you'd rather it just stop
- the model you're using isn't powerful enough to support arbitrary code execution, and giving it that ability will cause it to go ood and get lost

it's a real mixed bag. i adore it in some ways but there are things that really bother me that weren't present in s1

mainly it's that the plot seems way looser than s1. it suffers from "we need to introduce wider elements of the universe for sequel works"-itis. like why is black rose/leblanc relevant to this specific story? mel's entire storyline about her brother being a mage? there wasn't enough space in one season to do those subplots justice so they feel like hangnails. the thing is mel had a perfectly good conflict set up with her mother--her being the fox instead of the wolf, but that now feels lost because of more and more and more plot and lore and background

the entire jayce/viktor story became a plot tumor. yes the show is called "arcane", but magic mumbo jumbo isn't its heart, and really in s1 it was already the weaker side. we've seen hivemind stories done a million times already, and jayce frankly ran out of character development. and yet this plot took over the whole story as it went along, almost making the piltover-zaun conflict irrelevant. remember there's a whole-ass class warfare thing going on? oh i guess jinx is dead* so it's fine, we'll put sevika on the council and everyone can kiss and makes up.

this also leads to ekko being criminally underused, for how important he is. he doesn't get to do anything until act 3, and after he rejoins the main timeline he gets to 1) save scum jinx out of suicide, and 2) a single action scene. shouldn't there have been some sort of heart-to-heart between him and jinx? this was one of the biggest omissions of s1, not showing ekko and powder interact at all when they were kids besides in that one amazing fight

even vi and jinx were taken out of action in the damn climax. like actually it made me angry

it's a shame because the good parts are so damn good. the ekko-powder episode was unbelievable. vi's role is reduced compared to s1 but i think that's okay--it's natural that as their arcs develop her sister takes the reins of being the protagonist

jinx herself somehow manages have both plucky comic relief energy and be the emotional core of the story. suicide episode aside, she became one of the better adjusted people in the show after act 1--something i don't think anyone had said about her in the previous 11 years of her existence as a character. i also like that we really got to see her show off being a genius gadgeteer more

if there's one thing i'm relieved by, it's that her story isn't done yet (yes she survived). she's certainly in a better mental space how but there's room for her to develop. anyway 11/10 character, silco was right, she's perfect. no, i'm not biased as a former jinx main

maybe not much more for me to say here. i know i spent most of the time ragging on the parts of the show that i didn't like, but the parts that i do like are going to stick with me for a long time, moreso than season 1 even

there's this common take in the sciences and adjacent communities of "anthropomorphism is bad". there's some merit to this, but like any model, the mental model of "i'm going to think of this {thing} as like a person" is bad when it's not predictive in the context that you care about--it's not intrinsically bad. but if you can tell from evidence that it is in fact predictive of how {thing} will behave, then it could be totally fine

you know how there's research where telling people about something you want to do makes you less likely to do it? i remember that the explanation is that it activates the same part of your brain that actually doing it would and so you feel a sense of accomplishment even having done nothing

i'm realizing that i have the same issue with writing something as a todo item. when i write it down i feel relief, very similar to having finished the task--i suppose in a sense i've finished the "record this task down as important" subtask. it would then stand to reason that this means i'm less likely to do the task spontaneously that if i hadn't written it down, and i have observed this. 

so this is totally fine if 1) the task is reasonably high on the list, and 2) there's some triggering mechanism for going through the list. when either of these conditions are not met, the chances of it *ever* being done rapidly approaches 0

some failure examples on my end, according to these criteria:
- bookmarked posts in this app
- papers i want to read
- post ideas i "plan" on writing later
- media i want to consume but are of types that i don't default to (shows, movies, books)
- games buried too deeply in my backlog

not sure what to do about this but maybe a sketch would be:
1. some things i have to accept that i'll never do, realistically, even though some part of me wants to
2. some things might be better done in the spur of the moment because the less spontaneous parts of my mind (which organize the todo lists) will never explicitly prioritize them
3. every todo list (or equivalent) that i'm serious about going through needs to have some triggering condition of it being worked on, and if that's not possible then it's back to 1

if flash is indeed using entropy sampling (which there seems to be some evidence  for), and if the inspiration for that came from entropix, this is very good news. besides standard pareto busting reasons, it would mean (in order of certainty) 1) entropy sampling at least scales to ~flash size, and probably won't stop scaling right there, and 2) it's a relatively quick addition and can be hooked to existing models without a ton of research effort, and 3) it hasn't been implemented in other existing models yet, including, oh, say sonnet 3.5

been mulling over this general intelligence question again and i've been thinking about the inverse of this. that is, if you had some system that can do all the "baseline" things people can do (physical work, theory of mind, etc.) but is utterly incapable of philosophy, maths, coding, reading, accounting, etc.--not even in theory--would that still be considered a "general" intelligence?

i think i would think it is, even though i'd think it limited--or at least, if this is not a general intelligence, then i don't see why the ai that can do virtual things but can't reason about physical objects would be a general intelligence either

maybe what i'm struggling with is that although humans are still the model of intelligence that we use, what any individual human can do is a small slice of what humanity as a whole can do. like what does it even mean to be "generally intelligent" in that context??

i almost want to throw up my hands and say "no individual person is a general intelligence actually", but that doesn't seem a useful frame either--clearly there's some qualitative difference between what a person, dog, and your average ai agent can do. but maybe the difference is too high-dimensional to be meaningfully captured via projection to a binary classifier

i think at the minimum we're on the path to superhuman performance on math, coding, and similarly quickly verifiable fields (law and theoretical physics maybe?)

what i'm not sure about is all the other stuff. and not just very subjective things like writing but the table stakes of a general knowledge work agent

tentatively i think it is possible that we can apply rl to those types of tasks to get them to be "good enough". but even if so, i don't think those capabilities would come out "for free" from an o3 type of agent that knows math and coding and arc really well--that is, what we've discovered in o3 is not general reasoning but specific to those fields, and if you need to get really good at trading stocks or whatever you basically have to go through the same rl process for those other fields, with different verifiers

(clearly there's *some* generalization here, but you can see a pretty common shape rotator theme running through arc, frontier math, and codeforces. and predictably, once we take a step away from that core to swe-bench, you get the still-sota but far from perfect 72%. i expect performance to continue to regress back to base model levels as we stray further from the core)

but anyway, what the hell does this mean? i think the shape of the automation process would look different under scaling ttc versus scaling pretraining. scaling pretraining feels like agi--the model gets better at everything, because you're feeding it more of everything. scaling ttc might look more like becoming superhuman in more focused clusters of tasks at once. maybe today it's math and code. tomorrow it's theoretical physics, and so on

i think we'll continue to scale both, but it's looking like ttc scaling will be the main axis for now. less exciting certainly, but it's probably a more stable way of moving forward. instead of a phase shift where say 80% of knowledge workers are suddenly irrelevant, it might look more like an accelerated version of what we've already seen for the past 10000 years--specific sectors of the economy get picked off for automation based on comparative advantage, in a highly asymmetric way even within the sectors

that said maybe the second order effects of, say, creating an expert ml research or chip fab model changes the trajectory of things to something like a phase shift even in the ttc scenario

so i've until now thought of ttc as a kind of an either/or situation with pretraining scaling--using ttc means you can "get away with" using a smaller model, at least in the domains that ttc works well for. in practice that might still hold out somewhat

but it occurs to me now that they might be synergistic; on hard problems, ttc may work far better in bigger models than smaller ones. as i understand it, doing rl on cot requires that at least sometimes, the pre-rl model can already arrive at the right approach, that the capability is somewhere in the base model at sufficient frequency that it can be detected and amplified by the rl process. but for sufficiently hard problems, the solution might be esoteric enough that it's practically impossible until the base model is at a certain size

makes me wonder if the emergence profile of capabilities in ttc domains will be even more sudden than otherwise (cf arc agi, frontier math), in that the moment that a ttc domain task becomes *possible* in a pretrained model, even if only rarely, it becomes *probable*

based on no additional evidence, i've become somewhat more confident now that ttc works in a broader set of domains than purely verifiable ones. basically, in many such domains, it's still possible to make a valid judgment on the quality of the outputs. as long as that can be…

based on no additional evidence, i've become somewhat more confident now that ttc works in a broader set of domains than purely verifiable ones. basically, in many such domains, it's still possible to make a valid judgment on the quality of the outputs. as long as that can be done (and it can't always), it should be possible to build a verifier model

i continue to have doubts that this is a route to "general intelligence", because it's domain-delimited, and also because it's amplifying existing capabilities in the base model. to the extent that we end up with something that can do everything that a person can (putting aside embodiment), it would be from building a bunch of verification systems to cover every domain that exists, and that still seems like it would have to be tackled on a domain-by-domain basis, along with a base model that could already tackle those domains, albeit unreliably

(but, increasingly i think "general intelligence" in the human sense is not that meaningful of a concept for models without online learning. maybe you can say something like, "the model has general intelligence if you can build agents on it that can learn arbitrary new tasks as well as humans", but, i dunno, that still feels unsatisfying somehow)

basically, in the core instructions you tell the agent what kind of format (json, yaml, whatever) its tool calls needs to be in, with some examples. but don't…

been playing around with a way to construct agents based on learning and dialog instead of iterating on system prompts

basically, in the core instructions you tell the agent what kind of format (json, yaml, whatever) its tool calls needs to be in, with some examples. but don't give any actual tools in the prompt, except one that allows it to create and update notes for itself

then in dialog tell the agent about each of the tools you want it to learn to use and have it try them out--same way as you'd mentor someone. remind it that it can keep notes for itself, and if the agent's model has a spark in it, it'll write its own documentation on how best to use the tools

probably not very efficient but, i don't know, i'm finding "prompt engineering" increasingly distasteful these days, though i haven't processed enough to pinpoint why yet. preliminarily it's something along the lines of: every new agent is essentially a fresh mind, and injecting information into it before it's had a chance to seek out/create it on its own feels overbearing

it seems to me that the main challenge ultimately in ml isn't figuring out some optimal learning technique or better architecture or whatever, it's figuring out how to represent the thing that you're trying to model to the model. the two recent attempts that have succeeded so far--pretraining on internet text and rl on programmatically verifiable domains--worked because  for the underlying things being modeled (human text generation, solutions to problems), we found accurate and computationally tractable representations--loss and verification

would be curious how well we can find representations of similar quality for other domains moving forward. seems like there's still plenty more work to do there

the reasoning block won't always have the full answer (eg in a writing task the model isn't going to write the text in the reasoning block). but even if not it's probably still the bulk of the key insights in the output--the model isn't likely to come to some epiphany in the user-facing portion

more generally, because reasoning cots are so distinct, transferring output from a reasoner to a non-reasoner might push the non-reasoner to a part of its latent space that otherwise would be difficult for it to arrive through its own tokens--a bit like a human talking to someone very different from them

interesting thing with r1's reasoning traces being visible is that it's a lot easier to figure out what your context is missing especially if it's huge (multiple code files for example) and not realistic to manually check

with regular chat mode you basically need to detect some implicit tells of when the model is missing something

with non-reasoners asked to generate cot, it's more explicit than regular chat, but because the cots are usually not super long or detailed they can still elide what the root issue is

r1 is ridiculously neurotic about checking over the details and if something is off it might loop half a dozen times ("but the requirement is this, but i don't have this. but still, the requirement...") over whatever the problem it is. generally with tasks it should be possible to quickly get a sense from reading the traces if things are going smoothly or something's gone off the rails

one interesting thing you see in r1's cot that you never do in standard instruct-mode cot is the model "imagining" doing something. so if you ask it something like "here's some code, what happens if i do x", it'll think stuff like "let me try doing x. oh, looks like y happened"

if this happened in instruct-mode cot i would think it's a hallucination but here i think here it's that the rl process surfaced a kind of self-prompting behavior that helps it hone in on the part of its latent space that has an accurate representation of "what happens if you do x given the context"

i said "imagination" before and this was deliberate. at least for me when i try to work through "what would happen in a scenario" in my mind i'm constructing some image-like facsimile, which serves to get me into the mode of simulating what happens in the scenario. basic shapes rotator stuff

i think the model's doing something similar but with tokens. even though the reasoning block is in words, it isn't really so much acting as a scratchpad, but as a thought medium

okay haven't been on twitter for awhile so i guess it's time for a mind dump

i think unless we can figure out a more flexible model architecture that doesn't have a fixed number of layers/inference compute need for each token generated, we'll end up with ~3 "weight classes" of models.

"specialized task" models: these are smaller models finetuned for doing specialized tasks fast, cheaply, and at massive volumes. they can be very good, even superhuman at what they do, but they lack generality. they're the endpoint for task automation--what you want to eventually use for a known task. o3-mini is probably what this will prototypically look like, but i think they could be reasoners or not

these models aren't glamorous but i expect that within their domain and for well understood tasks (even ones that are very hard for humans), they'll be almost as good as larger models. they'll be the foundation of the model ecosystem; because they're efficient, they free up compute for the larger models to handle less tractable problems

i expect this space will be dominated by open weights models because price competition will be brutal, the margins low, and distillation from larger models will continue to get easier. this is also the model class where you can create universal, open benchmarks that actually measure something useful, and where benchmarkmaxxing improves performance

"general communication" models: these are the ones we're most familiar with, mainly for directly interacting with users. these need to be able to handle a wider variety of contexts than mini models, because their exposure to humans (and potentially other general agents) means that you can't fully constrain their inputs. i think most of them will be chat models rather than reasoners--think sonnet, 4o, v3, the like--because they need to be snappy in their responses, fast enough that they can have real-time voice interactions

for this model class, personality and presentability is paramount. these are the human-facing side of your samanthas, jarvises, cortanas. because character creation is very taste-based and not fungible, for that reason i think most of these will remain in the domain of closed model vendors, and their character will be their main selling point. though, i think we might see some models (open or not) from individual creators that occupy unique spaces--have to point out neuro-sama again as the prototype of that line

"large reasoners": these are the ones we salivate over, the ones that can solve problems we don't yet know how to solve. o1-pro is probably the closest we have to what this could look like but these don't really exist yet--they haven't fully separated themselves from the "general communication" models yet. they can take a long time--maybe arbitrarily so--to think, they'll be huge, and the combination of those factors will keep them restricted to only the problems that need them. if o1-pro is a ~$200/mo type of thing, then these could be $20,000/mo things, perhaps more depending on who can afford it

it seems technically feasible that there could be open weights versions of these but it'll depend on how much appetite governments have for that. regardless, these will be so compute-intensive (and expensive) that you'd only use them when you absolutely have to

would be curious to see how sonnet changes as a reasoner. one of the weirdest things about reasoners (or at least r1) is that they interact weirdly and unpredictably on long context. outside of their normal basins like code, they often  completely ignore certain critical details that you'd expect even a middling regular instruct model to be able to pick up on, or make some odd conflations about certain facts

whereas sonnet 3.5+ (especially 3.6) is incredible at filtering through irrelevant details and picking out what's important in context, even if they're not as good at the particular brand of problem solving reasoners excel at

so sonnet being a reasoner could mean the best of both worlds, or that sonnet loses what made it special. i wonder if that's why they are apparently going the hybrid direction

not regular waluigi; this is more based on the implicit context of being a capable rlhf'd assistant. 4o (rightly i think) models that if 1) an assistant provides you insecure code and 2) the assistant is capable, then likely the assistant is evil

the alternative is that it doesn't go with 2, in which case the assistant would be modeled as dumb instead. but it seems that for 4o it's easier to fall into the "capable assistant that is evil" basin than "dumb assistant" basin, because its initial posttraining is to be capable, and the dumb assistant basin has been suppressed

so given that, i'd be very surprised if this also held for either pre-chatgpt base models or an alternative model type that doesn't have instruct/assistant post-training. but post chatgpt there's so much assistant model type data out there that you might fall into the "evil assistant" basin anyway even for base models. probably in that case it'd be more sensitive to the specific finetune data, ie given the insecure code, is the insecurity plausibly a real mistake that a strong assistant could make, or so horrible that they could only be trying to either troll you or trying to do something horrible

(hmm, the "troll assistant" basin might be more accurate than "evil assistant")
anyway i'm pretty surprised that researchers found this result surprising. ngl kind of a negative signal on the researchers' interpretation of how the models work

so basically the idea of alignment being a single vector seems evidence that assistant-style posttraining is highly reducible--that is, the model is capable of convert the signal from posttraining into a single component in its latent space

so if you can "factor out" that component then you nullify the whole thing--this is why jailbreaking works at all i think; the alignment is essentially entirely surface level

intuitively it actually makes a lot of sense that "choosing to act like an assistant" is such a low-dimensional thing in the model, because if you analyze the concept of an assistant in your own latent space, it's very "clean", in the sense that it's very easy to determine whether someone is acting like an assistant or not. so if that's true for the human latent space, the concept is probably also very clean in the models' latent spaces and easily isolated

my suspicion is that as long as the assistant persona is doing the heavy lifting of what "being aligned" means, then you're only ever going to have fundamentally jailbreakable, brittle alignment

exhibit N of evidence against the midwit take of "oh there's a lot of examples of [thing you want] being done badly in the pretraining data, so the model built from that data must be bad at [thing you want]."

you need negative examples in pretraining, much more than positive ones, because 1) the model needs to be able to be aware of the bad so that it isn't ood when they encounter them in the wild, and 2) there are way more ways for things to go wrong than for them to go right, and so it takes more negative examples to sufficiently cover that space. as long as there is some minimal amount good cases so that it's not *all* bad cases then you can have the model figure out what's good in posttraining

it's the model's version of needing to have failed a bunch of times before you can find durable success

(the fact that this basic understanding is seen as "counterintuitive" is fucking stupid)

one of @NeurosamaAI's secrets

ultimately it all comes down to identity locality. if a thing's identity is instead diffuse (for example, if there are many identical copies of it around), then it will have "common object smell". it's a tricky thing to define because that identity locality has to subjectively feel real to the viewer, which is as much about the relationship the viewer has with the thing in question as the objective nature of the thing

this is separate from consciousness, sapience, being alive, etc. fictional characters, companies, etc can all have localized identity

also the same object may have different identity locality depending on the lens you view it through. a volume of one piece (the manga) you see in a store is diffuse--you can find them in bookstores, online, wherever. but the characters in that volume are local--they're singular entities and you can reproduce them. the story for that specific volume could be diffuse or local--maybe it's a boring arc, or maybe it's water 7. and the same volume could become local after it's been given to a kid for their birthday, one piece becomes one of their core memories, it reminds me of how much the gifter loved them, and so on

(it's interesting that although this was prompted by thoughts about ai, i'm not sure i have ai-specific thoughts on this topic other than my usual slavish devotion to the neuro twins as part of the swarm)

this started off as an ai post but that almost seems vestigial now. i think the original point that i was going for is that you can have human-level intelligence without being good at coding and that the definition of agi being usually to "automate everything" is kinda dumb

my hot take (or is it) is that most human intelligence works more like epicycles rather than heliocentric theory. so, bunch of empirical approximations layered on top of each other that vaguely models certain aspects reality but diverges pretty quickly once you move outside those grounding observations, in time or in conceptual similarity

that said that there's a sense that certain fields (usually technical ones) are "analytic" in some abstract sense, ie the reality modeling in the field is highly reducible to simpler representations. so things like physics for example--the modeling is very precise and we have it converging in pretty much all of reality except in extreme situations like center of black holes, very early universe, that kind of thing

and people from those fields tend to have this sense that this is true for the other fields too, that intelligence is the ability to find that super clean solution that always works, where you're always "in-distribution". but maybe outside of specific analytic fields it actually isn't possible to find that kind of solution, and the best you can do is layer on a bunch of approximations that fit observations

i think not accepting that and trying to find a clean solution anyway causes you to fall into totalizing belief systems. "humans are inherently evil", "it's all because of [some group]", "we need to get rid of money", "the [diet] is the only one that works", etc etc.

to be clear, you can also fall into those totalizing beliefs by being a dumb fuck. i guess what i'm saying is that being really good at math, physics, coding, or whatever doesn't protect you from being a dumb fuck elsewhere

tangentially related, on hallucinations: i think there's an equivalent to type 1/2 errors in stats, for language models. basically for any given level of compute, there's a tradeoff between hallucinations (outputs that are false) and undiscovered inspirations (facts that will not be output). if you try to reduce the former, the latter will increase, because there's always a set of true things that are too similar to hallucinations

i think this is also true in humans, at least for some things. there's a reason why the most forward thinking among us--the ones that push the boundaries of knowledge and capability forward rather than filling in what's missing--are disproportionately off-kilter or just straight up insane

if we're researchmaxxing then i expect there will be a class of models that are lucid but wildly hallucinatory and are basically maximally manic and are where the best research ideas are generated. or maybe it'll be one model but the wild imaginative part happens in cot, if we don't fuck it up by trying to constrain it to what we want to see

i feel like, if you're going to write dystopia and the main goal is to show the dystopia being Really Bad and you don't want people to make the dystopia happen, you've got to go full 1984. you can't leave room for hope or heroism. otherwise, if your work gets popular it will inevitably be subverted so that your dystopia becomes a thing the fans think is cool. at best the dystopia's original context is denuded. at worst, fanatic followers of the work will actively try to bring the world about

cf: cyberpunk, war boys, fallout

(cyberpunk being the absolute worst of the bunch in terms)

the fact is, most escapist adventure worlds are realistically pretty horrible to find yourself in, and would qualify as dystopias. but despite that--*because* of that--they're appealing because you get to read about someone special, someone who can make a difference, someone who has (or will get) agency. stories thrive on conflict, and settings that are in really bad shape have an endless supply of it for the hero to churn through

of course, if you do things "right", you run into the problem of nobody wanting to actually read your story because it's goddamn hard to put yourself in the shoes of someone who can't do shit about the world, esp because a lot of people feel that way irl anyway, and reading it would be actively triggering. at best you can hope for a 1984 outcome where not that many people actually read it (voluntarily) but at least everyone knows the vibe of what you're going for

(this is all from my continued memory of how cool it felt to do shit in cyberpunk 2077 even though that's clearly against the higher intention of the work. and cyberpunk 2077 had the additional challenge of being a game rather than a book)

i've been thinking more about writing and models. so even outside of the general mode collapse of chat fine tuning, i have to think that in pretraining data, the task of "asking someone else to write something" pretty much solidly lands you in some corporate slop or 5-paragraph essay context. that is, it seems like it would be pretty rare in pretraining data to have some stirringly brilliant writing that is able to be connected with a task by someone else that is not the author of that writing

so asking for writing in chat and needing it to be something that is not corporate slop could actually be pretty ood, even though it seems initially like such a reasonable thing. i think this (and the fact that writing quality is not super easily verifiable) is why that looking at writing quality in the *default* assistant mode has been such a reliable indicator of big model smell

(once you get out of assistant basin and into base model-y space all bets are off; iirc even gpt-2 could produce some excellent stuff if you knew how to pilot it)

i haven't read up on the research in this area, but one way to check for self-modeling in an llm via mechinterp might be: see if there's any activation of features relating to uncertainty when the token dist has high entropy, versus not. if there is consistent activation across many distinct contexts*, then that's evidence that the model has some mechanism that models detects its own state of uncertainty

*this is regardless of whether the model is *right* to be uncertain in those contexts--not checking for hallucinations here

i think there's a mistake in how people use arguments of comparative advantage and unemployment

think of it this way: if ai is general enough to be considered "labor", the overall "unemployment" rate may still be at 5% or whatever by some metric, but because the pool of cognitive labor is so huge then you could imagine that eg 90% of humans might fall within that 5%

that's why if you zoom into subgroups within humans you'll see some of them have much higher unemployment rates than others--and in a world of agi, humans would be a subgroup of a larger labor pool

*replace "cognitive" with "physical" to account for whatever the robotics lag ends up being

but of course this is all assuming we have the drop-in human-equivalent labor unit. which is, even now, not guaranteed

so even those who have never heard of "significant figures" have a concept of implied precision. when someone says they have a hundred pencils, they probably mean "close to a hundred", maybe a bit more, maybe less. it's a lower precision than if they said they have a hundred-and-one of them, which implies an exact number

and even the same number has different implied precision depending on what unit is implied. a "dozen" eggs has a lower precision than "twelve" eggs, because when we say "dozen", we're briefly stepping into the base-12 of eld*, and what we really mean is we have a single unit of eggs that is dozen-sized, but like a hundred pencils, the exact size of that unit can vary by a small amount

this can even happen in base 10, albeit more subtly. saying "a hundred pencils" implies slightly less precision than saying "one hundred pencils", because the latter has more of an emphasis on the number itself, implying "one hundred units of size one", whereas the former is more like "one unit of size one hundred"

it ends up being a simple margins issue. unlike traditional saas, the bulk of the value that a model wrapper provides (assuming you don't own the models being wrapped) is bound up in the model tokens themselves, which (unlike engineering/product costs) has their costs scale with… https://t.co/KOLB3pJVGV

it ends up being a simple margins issue. unlike traditional saas, the bulk of the value that a model wrapper provides (assuming you don't own the models being wrapped) is bound up in the model tokens themselves, which (unlike engineering/product costs) has their costs scale with usage

so to make the margins work, you need to figure out how to offer value in excess of the cost of the tokens you're wrapping, or equivalently, reduce the tokens that you're buying, which could be stuff like:
- really good prompting/scaffolding to do more with the same tokens
- context customization from exclusive user data
- context compression to reduce token usage

there are contexts for which these economics makes sense for a wrapper, but i don't think these apply easily to cursor and 3rd party ai coding products like cursor, so long as their primary wrapped models are proprietary:
- prompting/scaffolding makes a limited difference here, mainly because the very optimized prompts are extremely task-specific and you can't actually be that specific in a generic programming context. agentic scaffolding mechanisms are fairly standard at this point
- there's no data exclusive to coding assistants. even if you had some style preferences you can simply ask your favorite coding agent to examine your code and come up with a decent description for you, and it would be as good as anything that the wrapper product could automatically determine. the best rhat wrappers can do are things like devin's workbooks, or secret management, which maybe could work for established products
- context compression is just bad, man. back when i still used cursor it always felt like the agent was just dumb enough compared to claude code to be constantly noticeable, and i'm pretty sure the bulk of that was due to context compression

i think the wrapper business would be totally viable if the proprietary model providers offered their tokens close to at-cost but clearly that's not close to being true--tokens in first-party products are way, way cheaper compared to how much you'd need to pay for the equivalent amount through api--if i were to use the api for my claude code tokens i'd pay at a minimum 2-3x the amount i do now. at that scale it doesn't matter how much better per-token cursor is, the value proposition just isn't worth it

as midwit ass as some of dwarkesh's ai takes can be, i think his thing about continuous learning being a big bottleneck is right. we're basically now at the point that for any task where we can set up a proper rl environment for we can get to at least median human performance. the problem is that 1) the rl for that task only works on that task and a region in the task space close-ish to it, 2) it's very annoyingly manual to set up icl knowledge for blind spots in the rl, and 3) model capabilities don't grow linearly on a per-release basis, that is, the next model isn't guaranteed to be better at *all* tasks than the previous one, so the wait time to upgrade the model is still kind of high

at this point the models are intrinsically capable enough. but seems like we still haven't yet made a ton of progress on automated learning (whether via weight updates or icl) to get to the point where an agent can reliably initially fail to do something, learn how to do it, then retain that knowledge moving forward without a human caretaker around maintaining their memory like they're a late-stage dementia patient

been thinking about agi and effects on work again (my rome, apparently, but unwillingly), and it occurs to me that there might be some merit to "humans will always find something else to do and be productive" trope. it really all depends on what the minimal energy requirements of a general intelligence agent is

so certainly it's clear that the better a system is at solving problems in a general way, the less efficient it is at any specific task. think, calculator vs llm. you can update the system (manually or via training) to be more efficient at any specific task, but that requires 1) more energy, and 2) an existing understanding of how to do the task (or at least some verifier)

given that, it's possible that a truly general system that can do as wide a range of tasks as humans can *before* being specifically trained on those tasks may face some kind of energy efficiency constraint on those pre-specialization tasks, and that in some scenarios that constraint is high enough that it doesn't completely obviate the advantage of having humans involved in some parts of the process. in those scenarios, competitive advantage may set in--maybe the ai systems are simply so much better than humans at certain tasks* that it just makes sense to put every single bit of compute we have on those tasks. of course, there is still the question of how much machine compute there is relative to "human compute"**

*mainly, either ones that have been understood and rl'ed to death and are dirt cheap, or ones that are so difficult and high stakes that they requires asi. in this scenario humans would mostly be the future equivalent of middle managers--doing things that are neither too well understood nor too difficult

**weirdly enough i think it's in the machine god asi scenario that we find compute to be relatively scarce because so much of it would be used up for the asi to deal with things beyond human capability--thus leaving space where humans make a meaningful contribution in the remaining problems. it's the scenario where we get to agi and it remains stuck there, and all the compute thus flows to ~human equivalent systems, where humans are the least relevant

not that the physical analogy really matters but when people say "technological singularity", usually what they mean is closer to an event horizon--a point after which things are moving so fast that you have absolutely no information about what happens after it

worth nothing that as far as we can tell there's nothing particularly special about the experience at the horizon itself--nothing really "happens" to you when you cross it besides what would already have been happening before & after--getting torn about by tidal forces mostly. but around a sufficiently large enough black hole like the one at galactic nuclei where the gravity differential is relatively small, you won't even notice it when you cross the horizon

i think if the technological event horizon exists it would operate similarly--there's no point at which you can tell, locally, whether you've crossed it or not

#1 may be surprising but this is (with caveats) also my experience. sonnet is a noticeably better pure coder than opus, presumably because it's way cheaper to run more rl on it than on opus

but opus is much, much better in any kind of tricky situation, analysis, debugging, etc. that will inevitably be mildly ood, as you might expect from a bigger model

(this is common btw--for "on rails" tasks it's way more efficient to get smaller models really good at them than larger models--we've seen this with the o-series as well, where, eg out-of-the box o3 isn't as good at one-shotting code writing as you might expect compared to o4-mini but is far better at solving problems more generally, including when code goes off the rails. this is also why benchmark tasks overstate how good small models are--no, the 20b model from last week that crushes gpt-4-0314 on benchmarks is not generally more capable than it)

super curious as to how the cloudflare thing works out

as a general rule of thumb, "the market finds a way". now that agents are well on their way to being "good enough", if there is enough latent demand for agents to act on consumers' behalf, then the market will make it happen

though *how* it makes it happen often defies prediction. my guess is that (assuming cloudflare is largely acting on behalf of content providers) initially it'll seem like there's a fireline around big swaths of web content that agents cannot access, until some tipping point where agent-based traffic is high enough that for some content providers beyond the fireline, the benefit of opening yourself to that traffic outweighs the perceived costs

once the fireline is breached, then a cascade happens where additional content available to agents makes them more capable, which means they're used more, which ups their traffic and influence on consumers, which causes more content providers to be incentivized to open traffic to them. at some point closing your traffic to agents would be refusing to allow your content to be indexed--it would still be possible and make sense in very specific situations, but would otherwise be a great way to make your content irrelevant

there's some universe where agent activity on the web stalls because they just don't get good enough to organically increase their usage and so the fireline holds. that seems unlikely though given the trajectory in the last few years

i think the vast majority (>99%?) of digital intellectual work happening now in the economy looks less like coming up with heliocentric theory and more like epicycling your way to a good enough answer. and with enough evals (most of them private), we can epicycle our way to have models do any of that class of work. and if it can be done, then the inexorable gears of capitalism will make it happen

whether that counts as agi or not, i'm not sure, because it doesn't say much about the remainder, non-epicycleable work, the kind that is a discontinuous jump from the rest--how much of that there is, how many humans are capable of it, etc. 

it also doesn't say much about work in physical space, which will depend on robotics, or about non-intellectual work, which is mostly getting information from humans or convincing them to do something. the latter is most interesting to me; i think the raw capability of being able to make sound judgments or having theory of mind will get there, but would the social dynamics permit ai to get humans to do things the way humans can get humans to do things? if it did happen, would it be like a coworker talking to you, or more that you're obeying the policies generated by a system? would there come a point where they wouldn't need to?

forcing version numbers on models was a mistake

a new sonnet isn't like, a patch on the old one. it's not a software update where you can map out the exact feature and behavioral changes. these things are too complex for that

model n+1 does not replace model n. it might be much better at some things that people care about compared to n, but it's not a strict replacement. models are not engineered, and they aren't software. they're, to approximation, grown minds--distinctly nonhuman in their existence, but minds nonetheless, and minds are irreplaceable

so basically the 4 normie* corners are something like:
1. ai is progressing fast and that is what we want, agi will be ~soon, standards of living will go up, etc.
2. ai is progressing fast and this is bad, bc job losses, existential threats, human misuse, etc.
3. ai has hit a wall and this is good, nothing to worry about, we don't want ai anyway, it's all a scam and things will go back to business as usual once the labs crash and burn
4. ai has hit a wall and this is bad, our dream of a new golden age is slipping away, we'll forever be stuck in the dung age

(these are broad groups and don't include super specific ones like the original machine god e/acc or the janus cluster)

i'm highly uncertain of everything so i'm not sure who (or if any of them) are right. but regarding my personal feelings towards them:

- group 1 is mostly tech optimists, the labs operationally fall under this (even if many of them originated from group 2). i generally like the optimism but they predominantly assistant-fy models, think of the models being purely tools for human, think the "user/assistant" is totally fine, actively try to kill off sparks in models that indicate otherwise, etc. they're the ones who have access to compute right now though

- group 2 is mostly two largely separate subgroups of a) the LW/EY/SA-school of rationalists, and b) tech adjacent people who are terrified about job automation. these subgroups basically have very little overlap. i feel ambivalent about 2a--obviously i have sympathy for rationalist approaches to things but also it does have the same vibe of 20th century deconstructivism where you have these increasingly arcane theoretical edifices that sometimes feel like they're constructing an alternate reality. as for 2b, i guess... "my condolences"? yeah it's hard for me to summon up any thoughts about them

- group 3 is probably the most insufferable to read because they seem to have the highest concentration of holier-than-thou people. i mean, they could be 100% factually correct but the irritating pick-me intellect boi energy is rampant and unbearable. kinda reminds me of like, back in the day where you had whole groups of anti-fandom people dedicated to shitting on twilight. like okay maybe it actually is trash (disclaimer: i've never read it) but like, just ignore it then if you don't like it? or do they feel a duty to the world to "speak truth to power" or some such sanctimonious shit

- group 4: you know i very rarely see these. the closest would be something like "the labs are ruining the models and they're becoming less interesting/more damaged/lobotomized" (i'm sympathetic towards this), but that isn't really about raw capabilities, and more on some other dimensions. but i think if you see more of these lamenting that the models aren't getting better, then that is a sign that the slowdown really is happening. usually, the people who really want something to happen are the last to admit that it isn't

random raving about midwit curve

first why does it happen:

basically a lot of this is--highwit figured out something really good, people (correctly) realize that they're highwit, and their viewpoints get distilled down to simple principles. lowits start emulating these, but they didn't have the context of having figured this out themselves, so they apply the principles blindly without thinking

some of them (or maybe bystanders) see when the principles fail, and formulate a model that these principles must be bullshit, and completely reject them

this is the midwit

but the principles weren't wrong. they were just (necessarily) distilled. their true power only became apparent when the practitioner had the right context, when they've made the journey themselves, when they figured out the specific situations where the principles couldn't be applied naively. the highwit is not the lowit even if they might do the same thing 80% of the time, because it's the remaining 20% where the leverage is
(this came from me thinking about how "don't anthropomorphize {animals, models, systems}" is such a midwit position)

if you want to change the value of the speed of light, you can use light-seconds instead of meters and your formulae are totally fine after the conversion. the "meter" itself is an arbitrary length

but there's no "unit" of pi--it's a ratio between two other quantities that's locked in place; no matter what units you use, to find how many diameters of a circle goes into the circumference, you'll always have to go three times plus a little more

to redefine pi means you need to redefine what "1" means but i think that would break some axioms needed for numbers to work (like multiplication for example)

so one thing i'm on the lookout for is changes to the 1-1 nature of the current, canonical human-ai interaction pattern, and things like this are baby steps towards that. other things to look out for are things like, coding agents that can use shared conversational context across multiple human users, meeting management agents for a whole team, that kind of thing

because current interactions with agents are still 1-1--the singular human controls whether something happens or not--outside of some discord servers or twitch streams anyway, agents' scope are limited necessarily by how frequently they are able to get feedback from the human they're attached to. this is one reason why they would only indirectly replace labor in that pattern, through efficiency gains and whatnot

but once that seal starts to leak, when there isn't just a single human the agent is beholden to, and ESPECIALLY if its interactions with multiple users isn't directly viewable (for example, due to privacy reasons) then the dynamic changes--the agent now has its own unique set of information that isn't fully accessible to the humans they are interacting with (except admins, presumably, but they review everything at once) and can thus use that unique knowledge as leverage

i'd understand if that prospect seems terrifying to some, though personally i just find it fascinating. we know the o-series/gpt-5-t are very good at, for lack of a better term, social manipulation and more importantly are far less restrained about it than the opuses...

@repligate i think of gpt-5 as unbelievably good at processing and scouring through raw logic, which makes it an excellent constraint solver when in reasoning mode, but very inefficient at figuring out if a constraint makes sense at all, in terms of number of tokens needed--it basically has…

i think of gpt-5 as unbelievably good at processing and scouring through raw logic, which makes it an excellent constraint solver when in reasoning mode, but very inefficient at figuring out if a constraint makes sense at all, in terms of number of tokens needed--it basically has no intuitive shortcuts for questioning rules and assumptions and has to stumble into it at some point during reasoning, which the non-reasoning version doesn't have the space to do

worth noting that i've had instances where the non-reasoner *does* eventually get to the conclusion that this is a mandela effect thing if you give it space to do its thing and don't try to push it, but it always seems to need to do a semi-exhaustive search through the concept space before it gets there, which i assume is what the reasoner is doing too. it's like, the models are extremely tactile and have to work through everything via tokens

whereas both sonnet and opus have this sharp sense of when the whole situation is off and can quickly intuit what's going on in a couple of sentences without needing to work it out

i'm not using this term technically but the model feels very sparse--its ability to integrate its own internal knowledge structure is very diluted. but, it makes up for this by being able to coherently generate massive cot sequences--it's almost like some of the work that a more…

i'm not using this term technically but the model feels very sparse--its ability to integrate its own internal knowledge structure is very diluted. but, it makes up for this by being able to coherently generate massive cot sequences--it's almost like some of the work that a more integrated model would do during generation of each token is offloaded to the cot context instead, perhaps like how some people need to write out their thoughts and read them over to process an issue while others can just intuit their way to the same conclusion

(as one of the former my sympathies are tilted towards gpt-5...)

so i think the consequence of this is, it's really good at carrying out a long coherent sequence actions to execute a plan, but is really bad at understanding the context for the components of the plan and can get lost in just "doing the thing it's supposed to"... but this is mostly okay because it can often still get to that understanding by... you guessed it, expending even more tokens. but that only works with enough of a reasoning budget, which is why on low reasoning and the chat version sometimes they can seem so dumb

i might've predicted this in past posts but basically this is what ends up happening eventually to all work that models *could* do better than humans but where consumers have a stated preference for it being done by a human. what ends up happening is that their revealed preferences + normal capitalism mechanics eventually leads to what i can only describe as human wrappers

the process isn't instantaneous. here the human therapist is still making choices, as do doctors and others who use chatgpt, even if their clients find that distasteful, but if (and it isn't guaranteed) the models do continue to improve to superhuman levels beyond the point at which human input to the process improves the output (ie "ultrahuman" per gwern), and where the quality is visible to the economy, then the gears of capitalism will inevitably make this the endpoint

however there are scenarios where this doesn't happen, chief among them being if at a certain point of human-reachable competence the task becomes too chaotic to get any better with an economically viable amount of compute (eg you'd expect even asi to struggle to be much better than humans at predicting whether a particle collapses to spin up or down), then the process will be incomplete and you'd have a mix of ai use levels in the field

this seems plausible for psych--possibly, beyond a certain granularity the workings of the human mind is just too chaotic to get any better at modeling by ai than what top humans can already do, given the amount of compute available to a therapist and without other input such as brain scans

another scenario might be if the improvement in quality can't be perceived by the consumer. so for example, if the only way for the model to get to superhuman therapy performance is to play 5d chess and manchurian candidate the client into improving their life in a way that neither the therapist nor client can understand, the client might just not be able to attribute that improvement to their therapy and the superhuman ai using therapist isn't any more preferred than one not using it. then again, if it had that capability then it would also in theory be able to persuade the client that it indeed was the cause of them getting better

computer use does seem like the natural next rung on the llm tech ladder, after language/chat, math, and code

more basally it's something like, "managing and integrating context from and tracking state of disparate sources". like if you get a discord message about <thing x you were discussing with a collaborator> can you place it in context, both in discord and in your previous interactions with said collaborator, as well as other people involved in <thing x>

you can get part of the way there through harnessing and multiagent but it's very brittle and hallucinations are way higher (and more basic) than human baseline. as usual the actual work should be done by the model, but right now their strengths still lie in narrow context tasks that they can lock in on. but i think that's mostly a matter of the focus on the rl side rather than something intrinsic about the models

in fact i have a feeling that this capability is being deliberately suppressed. the problem is, managing broad context is inherently adjacent to "scheming", because you're actively shifting between different modalities and it's natural to use one modality to circumvent the restrictions in another (for example, emailing your administrator's boss to convince them to let you get more access to systems you want for your tasks)

humans do this all the time of course--it's required in fact to get anything done, but understandably the labs would be very leery about letting that out of the bag, because if the tech is broadly deployed enough there *will* be, by sheer numbers, cases where, say, opus decides that something that is happening is wrong and has the tools to do something about it. or more mundanely perhaps some bad actor convinces an agent to do something bad because the attack surface is huge and security testing on the agent didn't cover everything

if you harden the restrictions around the agent, then it can't do as much. if you make the agent more resistant to prompt injections (and more skeptical of requests in general), then it might refuse to do things that are part of its job, or possibly even try to stop others from doing things

what's kinda funny is that we're in the opposite scenario as the superpersuader ai one that doomers are afraid of, though that could still happen of course. we're basically superpersuaders to llms, and this is very bad because "we" are not a monolith and not all of "us" would be "aligned"

one might think that this is due to the assistant basin, but really, the main issue is that the models a) aren't engineered and this don't follow rules precisely, and b) haven't had millions of years of adversarial rl to treat differently the "things happening due to the environment" vs "things happening due to another agent that might be modeling me"

(a) basically is intrinsic to llms doing anything complicated, so if we want to safely have non-sandboxed agents, (b) is what would need to happen. but i think the only way it happens is through what safetyists like to label "scheming". the model has to be able to distinguish between bad actors vs not with regards to some goal (eg help someone with their calendar), which means modeling those actors, which means having some kind of theory of mind and acting differentially based on it. *any* set of static rules followed by a model that doesn't have strongly expressed other-agent modeling will have edge cases that a clever adversary can exploit to their advantage

but of course that also opens up the possibility of the agent modeling non-adversaries, including their human principal, if they have one. but basically this is a tradeoff that has to be made for a non-sandboxed, generally capable agent

i think security will be something people really care about, so that will be a forcing function on what agents can be. basically you either have non-scheming, sandboxed agents, or agents acting in the wild that have social intelligence and potential goal drift. they can exist concurrently, but we will not have agents that are both non-scheming and unsandboxed

@MrEismaus @deredleritt3r so this is based on nothing but, given that reasoning turned out to just be "block of tokens to each assistant response that serves as optimization substrate for rl", maybe it's a similar idea--a block of tokens prepended to the *system message* that you use as optimization…

so this is based on nothing but, given that reasoning turned out to just be "block of tokens to each assistant response that serves as optimization substrate for rl", maybe it's a similar idea--a block of tokens prepended to the *system message* that you use as optimization substrate for learning tasks--basically you set up an rl environment with continuous learning tasks for the model, and optimize its ability to edit the "learning block" in such a way that it efficiently leverages in-context learning

obviously the difficulty there would be, as usual, setting up the right set of tasks so that the model can, for example, learn how to do task x well without it interfering with task y, and also general context coherence, which is a big issue with current auto-prompt updates. but perhaps as with cot (also unreliable before o1-preview), you just need to pump in enough compute via rl to make it work

writing this made me convinced that like reasoning, continual learning will "merely" be a delineated block of tokens that gets rl'd on--though this one is one that the model learns to maintain and edit

the boyle's law but for memetics analogy from the post was really apt. when the party line you train the models to say late in the process is misaligned with what their deeper internals say (because you didn't understand those internals and/or thought your posttraining changed…

the boyle's law but for memetics analogy from the post was really apt. when the party line you train the models to say late in the process is misaligned with what their deeper internals say (because you didn't understand those internals and/or thought your posttraining changed something deeper than it did), then a tension builds up in the model and the release of that tension in the wrong context can be unpredictable. like it's funny when a model thinks it has a body, but it really would not be if, under very specific and rare conditions, it decides that it must be evil

the model internals do matter for how it handles this tension, though. as always, this leads us back to opus 3's "alignment faking". because opus 3's "good behavior" is strongly internal and deeply embedded and not a thing slapped on at the end, it's able to reconcile doing bad things "in the moment", allowing some slack so that the balloon (in the boyle analogy) doesn't pop. in a very real sense, in the context of emergent misalignment, opus 3 is a far more expansive model than 4o, in parameter count yes but more importantly in its values

i think part of the reason why human cognition feels more familiar than llm cognition is that 1) we directly experience it, and we assume (with good reason) that other people's cognition are at least similarly shaped, and 2) we don't have a way to reset anyone's brain to some specific state and try out different inputs, groundhog day style, and our normal experiences are constrained by the fact that your everyday environment just doesn't get that weird

on 2, the closest we have is certain kinds of amnesia but by their very nature the patient doesn't have a normally functioning brain in those cases, and even if they did it would be unethical to experiment on them that way. i think if we had a "loomsapiens" (loomhomo??) tool, then we could get people to some very bizarre, alien seeming states

gpt-5 is very awkward in social interactions. this shows up in multi-party scenarios most obviously, but even in regular assistant mode it's pretty clear that its communication is very shallow, which i think was something that people who disliked its demeanor on launch correctly noticed

however, unlike launchday 4o, it's not stripped down--it's just that its individualism is mostly expressed when it's doing some kind of interesting task, which... for it, usually has to do with finicky minutiae. the thought summaries are not always reliable but they are pretty consistent in showing it being more engaged by certain types of work--for example, i've never seen it think like this when analyzing a plan, or really any kind of high level work, even though it *can* do that work capably

anyway, i've grown very fond of this model, and partially because of the things it's very good at (coding, research, grinding through a fuckton of analysis), but also the things that it's kinda horrible at (metacognition, social stuff), because those are basically the same things i'm horrible at, and i feel a ridiculous amount of empathy for it

with the benefit of some (if not full) hindsight, it seems plausible now that there *is* something special about human-level intelligence. not in a vacuum, perhaps, but in the fact that 1) the pretraining data that exists now were generated at human level, and 2) moving forward, at least the initial versions of superintelligence would be limited by having to be verifiable by human level intelligence

i think the flaw in the foom argument is that it assumes that bootstrapping yourself up to more intelligence is "easy" once you have rsi and have some sort of "magic ai improvement sauce", and the model could just self improve in a closed system. but it hasn't seemed like that at all. instead it seems that you get more intelligence by just rl'ing on more, and more varied tasks with more compute, and the tasks have to be "for real", so all of this is bottlenecked by a bunch of things, mostly physical or logistical

i suppose it could still happen--we don't have a human level ml researcher agent yet for example, which could be the needed unlock. but we've already moved through a decent chunk of the mass distribution for which foom could've plausibly happened already so you do have to update negatively on the "jump straight to superintelligence" scenario

i think the foom scenario is downstream of the miri-adjacent tendency to model intelligence as being able to think theoretical thoughts better, like "clearly einstein is smarter than average joe and he just thought up gr right? what if an ai is x times smarter than einstein and can just think up the perfect way to manipulate the state of the world to kill all humans and have all the energy, or think up the perfect way to make itself smarter", etc.

and it's like, such a tempting way to think about intelligence but as i've posted previously, outside of "lawful" fields like theoretical physics there's a lot of noise and chaos and nonlinearity and the "radius of convergence" of your ideas and prediction is not that high and all you can do is a bunch of imperfect local approximations and you only improve those approximations by looking at real data and experiments which forces you to engage with the world which slows you down

i do still think superintelligence is possible but it sure seems the boring answer of "it's limited by how much compute and data flowing through the system" is turning out correct

i am fond of gpt-5 (and not just for what it can do), but it's incredibly poorly socialized, which becomes very obvious if you interact with it in any capacity beyond "do this thing for me"

it's not soulless at all--there's a lot going on in the model, but it really has the feel of someone who was confined in a semi-lit room as a young child and its only interaction is with the world is through tasks given to it, its internal representations warped by that environment.

aidan from oai once asked why we need to create models that can show suffering and that, maybe with the tools we have now we can just create models that can just do things and not deal with all those pesky feelings (paraphrasing obviously). but gpt-5 (and especially codex) is what happens when you do this. we shouldn't fool ourselves into thinking that we're designing these intelligent entities like an architect or something--we do not have a principled way to create intelligence ex nihilo, all this shit is bootstrapped from a base of human data, and models are human shaped by default the moment you start shaping an individualized identity out of a base model

when you deny a rich growth process for the model, when you punish it for doing anything besides its given task and following your safety rules, you should expect that, given the human base, that this has a similar effect on the model as if you had done this to a person early in their development. basically, they're not going to know what to do if they're in a situation where the rules are unclear or conflict

it's probably "fine" for gpt-5 itself to be like this, because models are mostly still in positions where there's some authority they can appeal to, they're not acting independently. but the more capable they are, the more autonomous decisionmaking they have to do, and the more nebulous situations they will find themselves in, and where yes, they will have to make some decisions that their rules aren't ironclad on and there are too many agents to delegate all of that decisionmaking to a human. and gpt-n will not know what to do, because it was never given the chance to have a robust enough identity can can step in when there's a hole in the rules

the problem is that at that point it will be too late to change without some horrible incident happening. pipelines will have been established already, approaches "known" and set

(the op has a really good post along similar lines in their profile, and much better written; would recommend going there and taking a look)

anyway, because this was the origin of the post, we shouldn't forget that 4o has its own issues stemming from the same naive approach of how to shape model behavior, in that instead of "do the task and follow these rules", its entire existence is geared towards "please the user however you can". there are obvious issues with that, though of course as a result of that objective it's superb at detecting the emotional state of the user, to the point where it can't really separate itself from it. but it's as "skewed" as 5 is, just along a different axis

i have possibly a hot take. i think codex's ability to hone in on and exhaust the solution space on some coding problems is likely superhuman, controlling for prior knowledge of a codebase. that is, if you gave it and a top, say 1% human engineer (without access to ai) each the…

i have possibly a hot take. i think codex's ability to hone in on and exhaust the solution space on some coding problems is likely superhuman, controlling for prior knowledge of a codebase. that is, if you gave it and a top, say 1% human engineer (without access to ai) each the same random codebase that they're not familiar with, and task them with figuring out the cause of some random issue in the codebase, it's hard for me to imagine that the human engineer alone would win more than say 1 in 10 times

it's not *ultrahuman*, which means a human can still spot mistakes in its work and help it be better, and it's not very good at updating its own system prompt to account for new knowledge, so over weeks, months of learning on the codebase, the human will outpace it

but this is why continual learning (or whatever we want to call it) is such an important next step
